proposals.title: Constraining Surface Ocean DOC Production Pathways Using Inverse Modeling of Deep Ocean Removal Rates in the Gulf of Mexico
proposals.motivation: The biological carbon pump's efficiency depends on dissolved organic carbon (DOC) production and export, but the relative contributions of different surface production mechanisms (e.g., phytoplankton exudation, viral lysis, grazing) remain poorly quantified. This represents a major uncertainty in marine carbon cycling models. The deep ocean acts as a terminal sink for semi-labile DOC, and its removal rates, shaped by microbial community function and ambient oxygen levels, may encode information about the chemical nature and thus the source of the exported material.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed spatial and temporal patterns of deep-ocean DOC removal rates, particularly their relationship to oxygen gradients and nutrient availability, can be used to inversely model and constrain the dominant production pathways in overlying surface waters.
proposals.method: We will apply an inverse modeling framework to existing and new datasets from the Gulf of Mexico hypoxic zone and adjacent deep waters. The model will incorporate: 1) High-resolution vertical profiles of DOC concentration, Δ14C-DOC, and microbial extracellular enzyme activities (e.g., glucosidase, peptidase) as tracers of removal; 2) Co-located measurements of oxygen, nutrients (N, P), and water mass age tracers. The inverse problem will be solved using a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm to estimate the most probable combination of surface source fluxes (parameterized as distinct DOC 'types') that yields the best fit to the observed deep-ocean removal kinetics and their correlation with hypoxia.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel quantitative framework linking deep ocean biogeochemistry to surface processes, moving beyond correlation to mechanistic constraint. It will directly inform models of carbon export by partitioning DOC flux into specific biological production pathways. The focus on the Gulf of Mexico links this global carbon cycle question to Dr. Kirchman's ongoing research on hypoxia and nutrient dynamics in large river-dominated shelves.
proposals.novelty: While most studies track DOC from source to sink, this inverts the paradigm by using the sink's characteristics to diagnose the source. The explicit integration of hypoxia as a key modulator of removal kinetics, connecting to Dr. Kirchman's prior work, provides a novel environmental filter for the inverse model.

proposals.title: Allochthonous Carbon Quality Drives Bacterial Community Succession and Trophic Transfer Efficiency on the Northern Gulf of Mexico Shelf
proposals.motivation: Summer hypoxia on the Louisiana-Texas shelf is fueled by riverine nutrient loads, but the role of specific allochthonous carbon (C) compounds in structuring microbial communities and subsequent carbon flow remains unclear. Determining which terrestrial C compounds most strongly shift bacterial composition is critical for predicting how changes in watershed land use and climate-driven discharge patterns will alter coastal hypoxia and carbon cycling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that complex, nitrogen-poor allochthonous carbon compounds (e.g., lignin-derived aromatics) will induce the most pronounced shifts in bacterial community composition towards specialized clades, and reduce trophic transfer efficiency to protistan grazers compared to more labile, nitrogen-rich compounds.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard incubation experiments using water from the Mississippi River plume and adjacent shelf zones. Treatments will be amended with isotopically labeled (13C, 15N) model compounds representing key allochthonous C classes: lignin phenols, vascular plant carbohydrates, and protein-like dissolved organic matter. Response will be tracked via high-throughput 16S and 18S rRNA gene sequencing to quantify community shifts. Trophic transfer will be assessed by NanoSIMS analysis of 13C/15N enrichment in sorted bacterial and protistan cells, and carbon flow modeled using linear inverse modeling techniques applied to the experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link specific carbon source quality to microbial community assembly rules and carbon use efficiency in a major coastal dead zone. It will provide a mechanistic framework for incorporating organic matter chemistry into biogeochemical models of hypoxia, moving beyond bulk nutrient limitation paradigms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies bulk riverine carbon as important, this study uniquely combines advanced isotopic tracer techniques with community sequencing to pinpoint the chemical moieties responsible for microbial succession and constrained carbon flow, testing Liebig's law at the molecular level for heterotrophs.

proposals.title: Network Analysis of Microbial Interactions and Environmental Forcing in the Northern Gulf of Mexico Hypoxic Zone
proposals.motivation: The seasonal hypoxic 'dead zone' on the Louisiana-Texas shelf is a critical ecosystem stressor driven by nutrient loading and stratification. While Kirchman's prior work has quantified hypoxia extent and linked it to climate and nutrient drivers, the specific roles of microbial community interactions in mediating carbon turnover, nitrogen cycling, and oxygen consumption remain poorly resolved. Understanding these microbial network dynamics is essential for predicting system responses to changing nutrient management and climate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the structure and stability of bacterial co-occurrence networks, inferred from time-series community data, will shift predictably across gradients of hypoxia severity and nutrient availability, revealing key functional groups that either stabilize or destabilize the ecosystem under low-oxygen stress.
proposals.method: We will apply ecological network inference methods, specifically SparCC (Sparse Correlations for Compositional data) and MENA (Molecular Ecological Network Analysis), to 16S rRNA gene amplicon and environmental time-series data from the Gulf of Mexico hypoxic zone. Networks will be constructed for distinct biogeochemical zones (e.g., oxic, hypoxic, anoxic) and seasons. Node attributes (e.g., centrality, module membership) will be statistically linked to concurrent measurements of dissolved oxygen, nutrient concentrations (N, P, Si), and organic matter composition to identify environmental drivers of network topology.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond correlative descriptions of community composition to provide a mechanistic, interaction-based framework for understanding microbial ecology in hypoxia. It will identify putative keystone taxa and functional modules critical to carbon and nitrogen fluxes. The resulting models will enhance predictive capacity for how microbial networks, and thus biogeochemical cycling, may respond to future nutrient and climate scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis is established in microbial ecology, its application to the well-characterized but complex hypoxia problem in the Gulf of Mexico, integrating Kirchman's expertise in carbon cycling and nutrient dynamics, is novel. This approach directly tests Liebig's and Blackman's concepts of limitation at the network level, moving from single factors to interactive constraints.

proposals.title: Resolving Nutrient and Carbon Co-Limitation in Hypoxia Formation: A Controlled Mesocosm Study of the Northern Gulf of Mexico Shelf
proposals.motivation: The seasonal hypoxic zone in the northern Gulf of Mexico is a major ecological and economic issue driven by Mississippi River nutrient loads. Pre-2026 research, including Kirchman's work, highlights the roles of nitrogen and climate variability, but the potential co-limitation by carbon (organic matter) and its interaction with nitrogen under controlled conditions remains unresolved. Understanding these limitations is critical for predicting hypoxia sensitivity to changing nutrient management and climate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hypoxia development on the river-dominated shelf is co-limited by bioavailable dissolved organic carbon (DOC) and inorganic nitrogen, and that the carbon limitation becomes more pronounced under reduced nitrogen loading, altering microbial respiration pathways and oxygen drawdown rates.
proposals.method: We will deploy large-volume (~10,000 L) pelagic-benthic coupled mesocosms at a coastal facility, filled with filtered shelf water from the Louisiana Bight. Treatments will impose factorial gradients of nitrate (simulating reduced riverine N loads) and bioavailable DOC (e.g., algal exudate mimics). Each mesocosm will be a closed hydrological system with controlled temperature and light, simulating stratified summer conditions. We will track daily changes in dissolved oxygen, nutrient concentrations (N, P, Si), microbial community composition via 16S/18S rRNA gene sequencing, and rates of bacterial production and respiration using isotopic tracers (3H-leucine, O2 microelectrodes).
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test Liebig's law of the minimum for hypoxia drivers, quantifying the interaction between carbon and nutrient cycles. It will provide mechanistic, experimentally derived thresholds for hypoxia formation under varying nutrient scenarios, offering a more predictive framework for management beyond empirical river-N load models. The results will clarify the role of organic carbon in sustaining microbial respiration when nitrogen is limiting.
proposals.novelty: While field correlations and models exist, this proposal uses controlled, large-scale mesocosms to isolate and perturb carbon and nutrient limitations for hypoxia—a direct experimental test not feasible in the open Gulf. It integrates Kirchman's expertise in microbial nutrient cycling with a novel perturbation approach to move beyond correlation.

proposals.title: Genomic Drivers of Microbial Diversity and Resilience in the Seasonally Hypoxic Northern Gulf of Mexico
proposals.motivation: Seasonal hypoxia in the Northern Gulf of Mexico (NGOM) is a major stressor driven by nutrient loading from the Mississippi River, creating a dynamic and challenging environment for marine microbes. Understanding how microbial communities maintain diversity and function under such stress is critical for predicting ecosystem resilience, yet the genomic underpinnings of this diversity in field populations remain poorly characterized. This gap limits our ability to forecast microbial responses to expanding coastal hypoxia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that microbial diversity in the NGOM hypoxic zone is driven by genomic adaptations to fluctuating oxygen and nutrient regimes, with specific taxa possessing metabolic flexibility (e.g., facultative anaerobic pathways, nitrogen cycling genes) that confers resilience to hypoxia.
proposals.method: We will leverage newly available, high-quality metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) from the NGOM as references for mapping metagenomic and metatranscriptomic reads from a multi-year, spatially resolved time-series spanning oxic, hypoxic, and anoxic zones. Diversity drivers will be quantified by analyzing gene and taxon abundance patterns in relation to environmental parameters (oxygen, nutrients, salinity) using multivariate statistical models (e.g., RDA, Mantel tests). Specifically, we will employ genome-centric metagenomics to assess population-level genomic variation (e.g., single-nucleotide variants) and expression of key functional genes (e.g., for denitrification, sulfur cycling, carbon degradation) using tools like anvi'o and DRAM.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link specific genomic traits to microbial survival strategies in a major coastal dead zone, moving beyond community composition to a mechanistic, genome-resolved understanding of diversity maintenance. It will identify key genetic indicators of hypoxia adaptation and resilience, contributing to more predictive models of microbial biogeochemistry under deoxygenation stress.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described NGOM microbial community shifts with hypoxia, this study is novel in applying a curated, local genomic reference framework to dissect the drivers of *in situ* population diversity and functional redundancy at a genome-resolved level, integrating spatial, temporal, and genomic data.

proposals.title: Linking Genomic Traits to Hypoxia: A Microbial Functional Genomic Type (MFGT) Framework for the Northern Gulf of Mexico
proposals.motivation: Current biogeochemical models for coastal hypoxia, like the Northern Gulf of Mexico, often rely on coarse taxonomic groups (e.g., 'diatoms', 'cyanobacteria') with fixed parameterizations, limiting mechanistic understanding of microbial community responses to nutrient loading and oxygen depletion. A trait-based approach, analogous to Plant Functional Types in terrestrial ecology, is needed to scale from genomic potential to ecosystem-level fluxes of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. This is critical for predicting hypoxia dynamics under varying riverine nutrient inputs and climate conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: Defining Microbial Functional Genomic Types (MFGTs) based on key metabolic genes (e.g., for nitrogen assimilation, carbon fixation, and stress response) will provide a more mechanistic and predictive representation of microbial community function in ocean models, improving simulations of hypoxia development and recovery on the river-dominated shelf.
proposals.method: We will curate a reference database of genomic traits from sequenced marine isolates and metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) relevant to the Gulf. Using clustering algorithms (e.g., hierarchical clustering on principal components of gene presence/absence), we will define distinct MFGTs. Key traits (e.g., nitrate vs. ammonium transporter genes, RuBisCO form, oxidative stress genes) will be parameterized into growth and nutrient uptake functions for integration into a coupled physical-biogeochemical model (e.g., ROMS-Fennel). Model validation will use pre-2026 observational data from the Gulf hypoxia zone.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, modular genomic trait layer for ocean models, moving beyond taxonomy to a gene-centric representation of microbial function. It will directly improve the mechanistic basis for predicting hypoxia by linking community genomic potential to oxygen demand via organic matter remineralization. The MFGT framework will be a transferable tool for exploring other trait-based questions in marine biogeochemistry.
proposals.novelty: While trait-based approaches exist in microbial ecology, their formal integration as defined functional types into operational regional biogeochemical models for hypoxia prediction is novel. This bridges the gap between high-throughput genomics and ecosystem-scale modeling in a way that directly addresses the limitations of current model parameterizations for key microbial processes.

proposals.title: Functional Seascapes of Microbial Metabolism: Linking Single-Cell Enzyme Expression Patchiness to Hypoxia Dynamics on the Louisiana Shelf
proposals.motivation: The expansion of hypoxia in systems like the Gulf of Mexico is driven by microbial processing of riverine nutrients and organic matter, yet we lack a mechanistic framework linking microscale microbial physiology to ecosystem-scale biogeochemical outcomes. Traditional bulk measurements obscure the functional heterogeneity within microbial communities, which is critical for understanding the resilience and tipping points of carbon processing under varying oxygen regimes. This gap limits our ability to predict hypoxia responses to climate and land-use changes, as highlighted in recent work on the Mississippi River-influenced shelf.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial patterning of extracellular enzyme expression at the single-cell level—its patchiness, connectivity, and diversity—forms a predictive 'functional seascape' that governs the efficiency and trajectory of DOM degradation, thereby controlling oxygen consumption dynamics in the coastal hypoxic zone.
proposals.method: We will apply landscape ecology metrics (e.g., patch density, edge contrast, contagion) to single-cell enzyme activity data obtained via Bioorthogonal Non-canonical Amino Acid Tagging (BONCAT) and Fluorescently Labeled Enzyme Activity Probes (FLEAP) for key hydrolases (e.g., glucosidases, peptidases). Cells from stratified water column samples across the Louisiana shelf hypoxia gradient will be characterized as patches defined by their multi-enzyme expression profile. Using spatial statistics and network analysis, we will quantify how this intracellular functional landscape correlates with in-situ chemical gradients (DOM composition via FT-ICR MS, nutrients, oxygen) and modeled physical patchiness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, spatially explicit framework for predicting community-level biogeochemical rates from the emergent properties of single-cell physiology. It will directly link microbial functional heterogeneity to the development and variability of coastal hypoxia, offering a new mechanistic layer for biogeochemical models. The resulting 'functional seascape' maps will identify key microbial functional types and their connectivity, informing how nutrient management (Liebig's Law) might alter microbial network resilience.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely transposes the patch-matrix-corridor framework from landscape ecology to the intracellular functional space of microbial communities, a conceptual leap beyond mapping taxonomic diversity. It moves from describing *who is there* to quantitatively mapping *what they are doing in space* to predict ecosystem function.

proposals.title: Genomic Provenance of Marine Bacteria: Predicting Geographic Origin from Adaptive Gene Signatures in the Gulf of Mexico Hypoxic Zone
proposals.motivation: Understanding the sources and dispersal of microbial populations is critical for predicting biogeochemical responses to phenomena like hypoxia, but direct tracking is impossible. Current methods rely on correlating community composition with environmental parameters, lacking a direct, genome-based tracer of geographic provenance. This gap limits our ability to resolve the origins of key microbial taxa influencing nitrogen and carbon cycling in dynamic systems like the Gulf of Mexico shelf.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the genomic content of marine bacteria, specifically suites of genes adaptive to local environmental gradients (e.g., oxygen, nutrients, temperature), contains a predictable signature that can be used to infer the geographic location of a strain's isolation.
proposals.method: We will curate a global dataset of marine bacterial genomes with precise isolation coordinates. Genomic features will be extracted as (1) k-mer frequency spectra and (2) a curated panel of adaptive marker genes for nutrient transporters (e.g., for phosphate, iron, ammonium) and stress responses (e.g., to hypoxia, reactive oxygen species). A convolutional neural network will be trained on k-mer data, while a random forest classifier will use the adaptive marker panel. Models will be tested and refined using a focused sample set from the Gulf of Mexico's hypoxic zone and adjacent oxic waters.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, genome-based tool for microbial source attribution, directly linking genomic adaptation to geography. It will provide a new lens to investigate microbial connectivity and residency in the Gulf of Mexico, offering insights into whether hypoxic zone communities are locally adapted or advected from other shelf zones.
proposals.novelty: This approach adapts the forensic geochemical provenance framework to microbial genomics, moving beyond community correlation to individual genome prediction. It explicitly ties the concept of biogeochemical tracers (like nutrient utilization genes) used in Kirchman's work to a machine learning-based spatial localization model.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Contribution of In Situ Production and Degradation to Fine Organic Matter Pools in the Hypoxic Northern Gulf of Mexico
proposals.motivation: In river-dominated shelves like the northern Gulf of Mexico, fine organic matter (OM) is often assumed to be primarily terrestrial, fluvially imported, fueling microbial respiration and hypoxia. This paradigm underpins nutrient management strategies but overlooks potential in situ sources. A quantitative assessment of locally produced fine OM via degradation of larger particles or microbial reworking is critical for accurately modeling carbon cycling and oxygen demand in hypoxic zones.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant fraction of the fine, operationally defined dissolved and colloidal organic matter in the hypoxic region is produced in situ via the enzymatic degradation of particulate organic matter and microbial reprocessing, rather than being directly river-imported.
proposals.method: We will conduct cruises across the shelf during peak hypoxia, collecting water column profiles in stratified and mixed zones. Fine OM will be size-fractionated. We will apply compound-specific isotope analysis (CSIA) of amino acids and neutral sugars to differentiate marine vs. terrestrial sources and assess diagenetic state. Parallel incubation experiments with isotopically labeled (13C, 15N) phytoplankton and riverine POM will track the production rate and isotopic signature of fine OM generated via microbial degradation. Nutrient amendment experiments (N, P, Si) will test Liebig's law limitations on this production pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first process-rate measurements for in situ fine OM production in the Gulf's hypoxic zone, directly challenging the model of passive riverine transport. It will refine our understanding of the organic carbon budget and the microbial loop's role in sustaining oxygen demand. The results will inform biogeochemical models and nutrient reduction strategies by identifying an internal source of labile carbon.
proposals.novelty: While bulk isotopic studies suggest mixing, our approach uniquely combines CSIA with tracer experiments to directly quantify the *process* of fine OM formation from distinct precursors, moving beyond correlation to mechanistic proof of an internal source within the hypoxic system.

proposals.title: Linking Organic Matter Size Fractions to Specialized Microbial Consortia in the Northern Gulf of Mexico Hypoxic Zone
proposals.motivation: The formation and persistence of hypoxia in the northern Gulf of Mexico is driven by the microbial degradation of riverine organic matter (OM) and phytoplankton biomass, consuming oxygen. Pre-2026 work, including Kirchman's, has characterized bulk nutrient dynamics and hypoxia variability, but the specific microbial consortia responsible for processing different physical forms of OM (e.g., particulate, colloidal, dissolved) remain poorly defined. This gap limits our ability to predict the fate of carbon and the sensitivity of oxygen depletion to changing OM loads and composition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, size-fractionated pools of organic matter (high-molecular-weight colloidal, fine particulate, and sinking particulate) in the Gulf's hypoxic zone are degraded by specialized and phylogenetically distinct microbial consortia, whose activity is regulated by nutrient (N, P) availability as per Liebig's law.
proposals.method: We will collect water column profiles across the hypoxic zone on the Louisiana-Texas shelf. OM will be sequentially size-fractionated via cascade filtration (e.g., >3 µm, 0.2-3 µm, 1 kDa-0.2 µm). DNA will be extracted from biomass on filters and from the colloidal fraction via ultrafiltration. Microbial community composition will be analyzed via 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing (V4-V5 region) using Illumina MiSeq, with subsequent analysis via QIIME 2 (2019.4) for alpha/beta-diversity and identification of taxonomic biomarkers associated with each fraction. Co-occurrence network analysis (e.g., SPIEC-EASI) will be used to infer consortia. Incubation experiments with size-fractionated OM will link community structure to function under varying N/P ratios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, taxon-resolved link between OM physical form and its microbial processors in a major coastal dead zone, moving beyond bulk carbon measurements. It will test how nutrient limitation shapes these consortia, directly addressing concepts in 'Liebig’s Law and Haber’s Tragedy'. The results will improve models of hypoxia by identifying key microbial actors and their substrate preferences.
proposals.novelty: While microbial ecology of hypoxia is studied, simultaneously partitioning both the OM substrate continuum and the attached/free-living microbial communities to identify fraction-specific degraders is novel. Applying network analysis to link OM physical fractions to consortia structure represents a significant advance over correlating bulk community data with environmental parameters.

proposals.title: Top-Down Control of Microbial Abundance and Its Consequences for Hypoxia in a River-Dominated Shelf System
proposals.motivation: In coastal systems like the Gulf of Mexico, summer hypoxia is driven by microbial respiration fueled by riverine nutrients. Traditional models assume microbial abundance is a direct function of substrate supply and activity (bottom-up control). This overlooks top-down controls like viral lysis and protist predation, which can decouple abundance from activity, potentially altering the timing, magnitude, and spatial extent of oxygen depletion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling top-down mortality (viral lysis and predation) as the primary control on heterotrophic bacterial abundance, rather than assuming abundance is activity-driven, will significantly improve predictions of the spatial and temporal dynamics of hypoxia on the Louisiana-Texas shelf.
proposals.method: We will adapt a coupled physical-biogeochemical model (e.g., ROMS-NEMURO or a regional implementation) for the northern Gulf of Mexico. The existing microbial loop parameterization will be modified to include dynamic bacterial mortality terms. Viral lysis will be modeled using a dynamic host-phage interaction module, while predation will be linked to microzooplankton abundance from the model's food web. We will run model scenarios comparing the traditional activity-driven abundance formulation against the new top-down control formulation, using historical forcing data (river discharge, winds) from pre-2026 periods to simulate known hypoxic events.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic framework to assess how viral and predatory processes modulate the microbial carbon pump and oxygen demand in coastal hypoxic zones. It will yield a more nuanced understanding of hypoxia sensitivity to nutrient loads and climate variability, directly informing management strategies for the Gulf of Mexico 'dead zone'.
proposals.novelty: While top-down controls are recognized, they are rarely integrated as primary drivers in operational hypoxia forecast models. This proposal explicitly challenges the core modeling assumption that activity dictates abundance, a paradigm prevalent in pre-2026 shelf ecosystem models.

proposals.title: Nutrient Pulses and the Activation of the Rare Biosphere: A High-Resolution Study of Taxon-Specific Responses to Hypoxia on the Louisiana Shelf
proposals.motivation: The 'rare biosphere' represents a vast reservoir of microbial diversity, hypothesized to act as a seed bank that responds to changing environmental conditions. On river-dominated shelves like the northern Gulf of Mexico, seasonal hypoxia creates dynamic chemical gradients, but the specific nutrient pulses (e.g., nitrogen forms, organic matter) that trigger rare bacterial taxa to become active and abundant remain unknown. Understanding these triggers is critical for predicting microbial community responses to eutrophication and climate-driven changes in oxygen regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, low-concentration pulses of labile organic nitrogen compounds, co-occurring with the onset of seasonal hypoxia, serve as key environmental triggers that activate distinct subsets of rare bacterial taxa, leading to their transient dominance in the microbial community.
proposals.method: We will analyze a multi-year, high-resolution time-series of water column chemistry (nutrients, O2) and 16S rRNA gene sequences (DNA) and transcripts (RNA) from the Louisiana Hypoxia Zone. Using state-of-the-art bioinformatics (DADA2 for ASV calling, PICRUSt2 for functional inference), we will identify 'rare' taxa (ASVs <0.1% relative abundance in DNA) that become 'active and abundant' (>1% in RNA). We will employ multivariate statistical modeling (Canonical Correspondence Analysis, Random Forest regression) to correlate these taxon-specific activation events with precise environmental conditions, including nutrient ratios (N:P:Si), organic matter quality (from parallel metabolomics), and the rate of oxygen decline.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond describing community shifts to mechanistically link specific chemical triggers to the activation of individual rare taxa. It will provide a quantitative framework for predicting microbial succession during hypoxia. The findings will directly inform models of biogeochemical cycling by identifying which taxa and functions are mobilized under eutrophic, low-oxygen conditions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has cataloged community changes with hypoxia, this proposal uniquely integrates high-resolution molecular (RNA/DNA) and chemical data to perform a taxon-resolved, causative analysis of rare biosphere activation, testing Liebig's law at the level of individual microbial species.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Activity of the Rare Biosphere in a Seasonally Hypoxic Coastal Zone
proposals.motivation: The 'rare biosphere' of marine microbes is a vast reservoir of genomic diversity, but its functional role in biogeochemical cycles remains largely unknown due to methodological limitations. In coastal zones like the Gulf of Mexico, where seasonal hypoxia is driven by nutrient inputs and stratification, rare taxa may become active and play critical roles in carbon and nitrogen cycling during ecosystem transitions, a possibility that bulk community measurements cannot resolve.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, rare bacterial taxa exhibit significant transcriptional activity and distinct functional roles (e.g., in organic matter degradation or nitrogen metabolism) in response to the development of hypoxia on the Louisiana-Texas shelf.
proposals.method: We will collect water samples across defined biogeochemical zones (oxic, hypoxic, anoxic) during a summer cruise on the Texas-Louisiana shelf. Individual microbial cells will be isolated and barcoded using a 10x Genomics Chromium platform for single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq). Bioinformatic analysis will involve clustering cells using Seurat or Scanpy to identify taxonomic groups via phylogenetic placement of universal genes, followed by differential gene expression analysis to compare functional profiles (e.g., nitrification, denitrification, organic carbon hydrolysis) across oxygen zones and between rare and abundant populations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, single-cell resolution evidence of the metabolic activity and ecological function of rare marine bacteria within a dynamic, hypoxia-prone coastal system. It will test whether the rare biosphere acts as a functional reservoir that responds to environmental stress, thereby refining our understanding of microbial contributions to 'dead zone' biogeochemistry beyond Liebig's law of the minimum.
proposals.novelty: While Kirchman's prior work used bulk community metrics like a hypoxia index, applying scRNA-seq to a natural marine hypoxia gradient is novel and moves beyond correlative abundance data to directly measure the in situ activity of uncultivated, rare cells, linking their real-time function to ecosystem-scale processes.

proposals.title: Microfluidic Enrichment and Single-Cell Tracking of Rare Microbial Taxa in the Hypoxic Northern Gulf of Mexico
proposals.motivation: Summer hypoxia in the northern Gulf of Mexico is a major ecosystem stressor driven by nutrient loading from the Mississippi River. The microbial communities mediating carbon remineralization and nitrogen cycling in hypoxic zones are critical to its formation and persistence, but the growth dynamics and ecological roles of rare, low-abundance taxa within these communities remain largely unknown due to methodological limitations in isolating and culturing them from complex seawater.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rare microbial taxa enriched from hypoxic zone seawater using microfluidic devices will exhibit distinct growth responses to gradients of organic matter and nitrogen, and that their activity contributes disproportionately to oxygen consumption under substrate-amended conditions.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate microfluidic droplet generators and microwell arrays to encapsulate and culture single cells from water samples collected across oxygen gradients on the Louisiana-Texas shelf. The devices will integrate dielectrophoresis or affinity-based capture for pre-enrichment of target cells. Growth dynamics of encapsulated rare taxa (e.g., specific Bacteroidetes or Planctomycetes) will be tracked via time-lapse microscopy, with growth rates quantified using custom image analysis algorithms in Python (scikit-image, TrackPy). Subsequent single-cell genomics (multiple displacement amplification followed by 16S rRNA gene and metagenomic sequencing) will link phylogeny to function.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, cultivation-based measurements of growth rates and substrate responses for rare microbes from a major hypoxic zone. It will test Liebig's law of the minimum for individual taxa within a complex community and identify whether specific rare organisms act as keystone species in hypoxia development, moving beyond correlative 'dead' zone studies to mechanistic, individual-based understanding.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics has been used in biomedical contexts, its application for enriching and tracking the growth of uncultured, rare environmental microbes from a dynamic marine hypoxic system is novel. This approach bridges the gap between bulk biogeochemical measurements and single-cell activity in a key region of Dr. Kirchman's research.

proposals.title: Global Mapping of the Active Rare Biosphere: A Metagenomic Activity Framework Applied to Tara Oceans Time-Series Data
proposals.motivation: The microbial 'rare biosphere' constitutes a vast reservoir of genetic diversity with potential roles in ecosystem resilience and biogeochemical function, but its activity and ecological significance remain poorly characterized at a global scale. Pre-2026 studies, including Kirchman's work on hypoxia and nutrient dynamics in systems like the Gulf of Mexico, highlight that environmental stressors disproportionately impact microbial activity and community structure. A global, activity-centric analysis is needed to understand how this dormant microbial seed bank responds to and influences large-scale ocean processes like deoxygenation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the genomic potential within the global ocean's rare biosphere is transcriptionally active, and that this active rare fraction exhibits predictable spatial and temporal patterns linked to regional physicochemical gradients, such as oxygen and nutrient concentrations.
proposals.method: We will apply an 'activity framework' by analyzing paired metagenomic and metatranscriptomic data from the Tara Oceans and related time-series projects. The core method involves calculating per-genome or per-operational taxonomic unit (OTU) activity indices (e.g., transcript-to-gene read ratios) to distinguish active from dormant populations. We will use established bioinformatics pipelines (e.g., EBI Metagenomics, MG-RAST) for read processing, assembly, and gene calling, followed by statistical modeling (generalized additive models) to correlate active rare biosphere patterns with global environmental parameters like temperature, chlorophyll-a, nitrate, and dissolved oxygen.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first global maps of active versus dormant microbial diversity, directly linking the metabolic potential of the rare biosphere to in situ function. It will quantify the contribution of rare taxa to total community transcriptional profiles and identify environmental 'triggers' that activate specific rare lineages, thereby advancing from cataloging diversity to understanding its dynamic role in ocean biogeochemistry.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have described rare biosphere composition, this proposal uniquely scales Kirchman's activity-focused framework—developed for coastal hypoxia—to a global, temporally resolved context. The integration of metatranscriptomics with vast metagenomic catalogs to systematically map active rare biosphere patterns globally is a novel synthesis beyond current biogeographic surveys.

proposals.title: Activity-Based Rarity: Redefining Microbial Functional Significance in the Seasonally Hypoxic Northern Gulf of Mexico
proposals.motivation: Microbial ecology traditionally defines 'rare' biosphere members by low abundance, yet their functional roles in biogeochemical cycles, particularly during hypoxic events, remain ambiguous. In river-dominated shelves like the Northern Gulf of Mexico, hypoxia is driven by nutrient loading and microbial respiration, but the contribution of low-abundance yet highly active taxa is poorly constrained. This gap limits our predictive understanding of oxygen dynamics and nutrient (N) cycling under varying climate and anthropogenic forcing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant fraction of microbial activity driving oxygen consumption and nitrogen transformation in the Gulf's hypoxic zone is performed by taxa that are rare in abundance but high in per-cell activity, and that this 'active rare' community shifts predictably across oxic-hypoxic transition zones.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and new 16S rRNA and rRNA gene (i.e., 'rRNA:rDNA') amplicon datasets from the Gulf's water column across hypoxia gradients to estimate in situ activity. Taxa will be categorized into an 'Activity Rarity Framework' (Abundant-Active, Abundant-Inactive, Rare-Active, Rare-Inactive) based on abundance (rDNA) and activity proxy (rRNA:rDNA ratio). This framework will be linked to geochemical profiles (O2, nutrients) and process rates (e.g., 15N-based nitrification/denitrification assays). Statistical modeling (e.g., Random Forest) will identify environmental drivers of the Rare-Active group.
proposals.contribution: This work will move microbial ecology beyond abundance-centric views by providing an operational, activity-based definition of rarity. It will directly quantify the functional significance of the rare biosphere in a major coastal hypoxia system, refining models of oxygen and nitrogen cycling. The framework will offer a generalizable approach for assessing microbial community responses to environmental perturbation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly decoupling activity from abundance to define functional rarity, a conceptual shift from standard rarity metrics. Applying this framework to the well-studied Gulf hypoxia problem challenges the assumed primacy of abundant taxa in driving biogeochemical fluxes.

proposals.title: Linking Rare Microbial Taxa to the Slow Degradation of Recalcitrant Dissolved Organic Matter in the Hypoxic Northern Gulf of Mexico
proposals.motivation: The recalcitrant dissolved organic matter (RDOM) pool is a major carbon reservoir in the ocean, yet its cycling remains poorly understood. In river-dominated shelves like the Northern Gulf of Mexico, hypoxia driven by nutrient loading may alter microbial community structure and function, potentially influencing the fate of RDOM. Determining if RDOM is truly inert or slowly degraded is critical for constraining carbon budgets and understanding feedbacks between eutrophication, hypoxia, and carbon sequestration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that RDOM in the hypoxic zone of the Northern Gulf of Mexico is slowly degraded by specialized, rare microbial taxa, and that the rate and extent of this degradation are influenced by the prevailing oxygen regime and nutrient stoichiometry.
proposals.method: We will conduct incubation experiments using water collected across oxygen gradients (oxic, hypoxic, anoxic zones) on the Louisiana-Texas shelf. RDOM will be isolated via solid-phase extraction from deep oceanic water as a representative recalcitrant pool. Amendments of this RDOM, along with 15N- and 13C-labeled substrates, will track its incorporation into biomass and respiration products. Microbial community dynamics will be analyzed via high-throughput 16S rRNA and metagenomic sequencing, with a focus on identifying low-abundance taxa (e.g., using the 'breakaway' R package for richness estimation) whose relative abundance correlates with RDOM degradation metrics. Nutrient analyses (N, P) will assess Liebig's law limitations.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the biological reactivity of the RDOM pool in a climatically sensitive region, providing empirical rate constants for models. It will identify specific microbial clades responsible for RDOM processing, moving beyond bulk community descriptions. The findings will integrate into our understanding of how human-driven nutrient loading and resulting hypoxia alter the marine carbon cycle.
proposals.novelty: While RDOM is often considered inert, this proposal explicitly links its degradation to the activity of rare biosphere members, a connection not yet demonstrated in the Gulf's hypoxic zone. It combines advanced isotopic tracing with cutting-edge microbial ecology statistical tools to move from correlation to mechanism.

proposals.title: Linking Microbial Community Succession to Recalcitrant DOM Production in the Northern Gulf of Mexico Hypoxic Zone
proposals.motivation: Recalcitrant dissolved organic matter (RDOM) is a major carbon reservoir in the ocean, influencing carbon sequestration and microbial food webs. In river-dominated shelves like the Northern Gulf of Mexico, hypoxia driven by nutrient loading fundamentally alters microbial metabolism, but the specific microbial taxa and consortia responsible for transforming labile organic matter into RDOM remain unidentified. This knowledge gap limits our ability to predict how coastal carbon cycling will respond to changing nutrient regimes and expanding hypoxia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, hypoxia-adapted microbial taxa and consortia, particularly those involved in the processing of nitrogen-rich organic matter, are key producers of RDOM in the Gulf of Mexico's hypoxic zone.
proposals.method: We will conduct incubation experiments with water from across the hypoxic zone, tracking DOM chemical composition via FT-ICR-MS and linking it to microbial community succession via 16S rRNA and metatranscriptomic sequencing. Using a nutrient amendment framework (inspired by 'Liebig’s Law and Haber’s Tragedy'), we will test how nitrogen and phosphorus limitation influence RDOM production. Network analysis (e.g., SPIEC-EASI or CoNet) will be used to identify co-occurring taxa and their statistical association with the accumulation of specific RDOM molecular formulae.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link specific microbial lineages and their expressed functions to the production of RDOM, moving beyond bulk community descriptions. It will provide a mechanistic understanding of how hypoxia and nutrient stoichiometry shape the microbial 'dead end' of carbon flow, refining carbon budget models for the Gulf of Mexico and similar systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized RDOM pools and microbial communities separately, this proposal integrates high-resolution molecular chemistry with network-based microbial ecology to identify causative agents, a non-obvious step given the complexity of microbial consortia and DOM chemistry.

proposals.title: Temperature, Nutrient Stoichiometry, and Hypoxia as Drivers of Recalcitrant Dissolved Organic Matter Production on the Northern Gulf of Mexico Shelf
proposals.motivation: The microbial production of recalcitrant dissolved organic matter (RDOM) is a critical but poorly constrained process in the marine carbon cycle, influencing carbon sequestration. On river-dominated shelves like the northern Gulf of Mexico, seasonal hypoxia driven by nutrient loading creates a dynamic environment where the interplay of oxygen, temperature, and nutrient stoichiometry (Liebig's Law) may fundamentally alter microbial carbon processing pathways. Understanding these controls is essential for predicting how coastal carbon cycling will respond to climate change and altered nutrient regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the microbial production of RDOM is maximized under conditions of moderate hypoxia and nitrogen limitation relative to phosphorus, which shifts microbial metabolism from growth efficiency to carbon oxidation and polymerization, with temperature modulating the rate and extent of this process.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard incubation experiments using water collected across the hypoxic zone and adjacent shelf regions of the northern Gulf of Mexico. Treatments will manipulate temperature, oxygen levels (oxic, hypoxic), and nutrient ratios (N:P) in a factorial design. RDOM production will be tracked via high-resolution liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) to characterize molecular composition and lability, alongside measurements of microbial community composition (16S/18S rRNA gene sequencing) and extracellular enzyme activities. Data will be analyzed using multivariate statistics (PERMANOVA, RDA) to link environmental drivers to RDOM molecular signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, process-based understanding of how key environmental stressors co-vary to control RDOM production in a major coastal system. It will directly link the biogeochemical concepts of Liebig's Law and hypoxia to the molecular composition of the carbon pool, offering predictive relationships for carbon sequestration potential under future scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described hypoxia patterns and nutrient impacts, this study uniquely integrates the three key variables—temperature, nutrient stoichiometry, and oxygen—in a controlled framework to test their interactive effects on the *production* (not just consumption) of RDOM, moving beyond correlative field observations.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Hypoxic Zone Dynamics in the Northern Gulf of Mexico: RDOM Composition as a Biosignature of Past Microbial Processing and Nutrient Regimes
proposals.motivation: The refractory dissolved organic matter (RDOM) pool in the ocean is a legacy of microbial processing, yet its potential as a biosignature to reconstruct past ocean conditions is largely untapped. This is critical for river-dominated shelves like the Northern Gulf of Mexico, where hypoxia is driven by complex interactions between nutrient loads (nitrogen) and microbial community responses. A method to infer historical microbial community structure and nutrient regimes from RDOM would provide a novel constraint on the long-term drivers of hypoxia beyond direct observational records.
proposals.hypothesis: The molecular and isotopic composition of RDOM in sediment cores from the Gulf of Mexico hypoxic zone encodes a record of past microbial community metabolic pathways, which can be quantitatively linked to historical nutrient regimes (e.g., nitrogen speciation) and the intensity of seasonal hypoxia.
proposals.method: We will analyze RDOM from dated sediment cores across the Louisiana-Texas shelf using ultrahigh-resolution mass spectrometry (FT-ICR MS) and isotopic analysis (δ¹⁵N, δ¹³C). The complex molecular data will be integrated with a 40-year record of hypoxia indices and riverine nutrient loads. We will employ a supervised machine learning framework, specifically a Random Forest regression model, to identify diagnostic molecular formulas and isotopic signatures associated with known historical periods of high/low hypoxia and varying nitrogen nutrient ratios. The trained model will then be applied to deeper core sections to infer pre-observational conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel paleo-proxy for shelf hypoxia based on RDOM biosignatures, moving beyond bulk geochemical indicators. It will provide a quantitative, microbial-process-informed reconstruction of nutrient-hypoxia coupling in the Gulf of Mexico, testing paradigms like Liebig's law in a historical context. The methodology will be a proof-of-concept for applying inverse modeling to RDOM to decode ocean biogeochemical history.
proposals.novelty: While RDOM is studied as a product of microbial activity, using its composition as an inverse model to reconstruct specific past microbial community functions and nutrient conditions on a dynamic shelf is a novel application. This approach directly connects molecular organic geochemistry with historical ecology and hypoxia dynamics.

proposals.title: Reconstructing the Microbial Carbon Pump's Role in Carbon Sequestration Across Glacial-Interglacial Cycles in the Gulf of Mexico
proposals.motivation: The role of the Microbial Carbon Pump (MCP) in converting labile dissolved organic matter (DOM) into refractory DOM (RDOM) is a key, yet poorly constrained, component of the biological carbon pump. Its contribution to long-term carbon storage during past climatic shifts, such as the transitions between glacial and interglacial periods characterized by varying riverine nutrient loads and shelf hypoxia, remains unknown. This gap limits our ability to model the ocean's carbon cycle response to climate forcing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the efficiency of the MCP in producing RDOM on the Gulf of Mexico shelf was enhanced during interglacial periods of high riverine nutrient discharge and seasonal hypoxia, leading to greater carbon sequestration in the deep ocean compared to glacial periods.
proposals.method: We will analyze sediment cores from the Louisiana-Texas shelf spanning the last glacial cycle. We will apply a novel multi-proxy approach: (1) using lignin phenol and δ13C-DOM signatures as paleo-proxies for terrestrial vs. marine RDOM sources and diagenetic state, (2) coupling these with established paleo-productivity (biogenic silica, alkenones) and hypoxia proxies (trace metals, benthic foraminifera), and (3) employing a statistical model (e.g., Bayesian mixing model) to quantify the relationship between reconstructed nutrient loading, hypoxia extent, and RDOM accumulation rates across defined temporal zones.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical reconstruction of MCP-driven carbon storage on a climatically sensitive river-dominated shelf, directly linking terrestrial nutrient inputs to long-term oceanic carbon sequestration. It will test the applicability of the MCP concept in paleo-oceanography and inform Earth system models on the climate sensitivity of this carbon pathway.
proposals.novelty: While Kirchman's prior work has examined modern MCP dynamics and hypoxia, this project uniquely scales the investigation across millennia, applying emerging RDOM molecular proxies to paleo-archives to test the MCP's role over full glacial-interglacial cycles, a temporal dimension previously unexplored for this mechanism.

proposals.title: Divergent Biogeochemical Legacies: Testing the Homogeneity of Microbial Selection Pressures in Arctic and Antarctic Marine Systems
proposals.motivation: Polar oceans are often grouped as low-temperature, seasonally light-limited ecosystems, but their underlying nutrient regimes and physical forcings differ profoundly. The Arctic is a river-dominated, semi-enclosed basin experiencing increasing terrestrial nutrient loads and severe seasonal hypoxia, while the Antarctic is an open, iron-limited system with minimal riverine input. Understanding if these differences create fundamentally distinct selection pressures on microbial communities is critical for predicting polar carbon cycling and ecosystem responses to climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the legacy of contrasting nutrient regimes (e.g., nitrogen vs. iron limitation) and light histories (ice-covered duration and spectral quality) selects for functionally distinct microbial assemblages and metabolic strategies in the Arctic and Antarctic, despite shared thermal constraints.
proposals.method: We will conduct a comparative analysis using existing and new field data from the Gulf of Mexico hypoxic zone (as an analog for river-influenced systems) and Antarctic coastal zones. The approach integrates: 1) High-throughput 16S rRNA and metagenomic sequencing of microbial communities across gradients of salinity, nutrients, and dissolved oxygen. 2) Application of a Hypoxia Index framework to quantify and compare the drivers of oxygen depletion. 3) Nutrient amendment bioassay experiments (N, Fe, Si) to identify limiting nutrients and assess microbial functional responses. 4) Statistical modeling (e.g., redundancy analysis, structural equation modeling) to disentangle the effects of nutrients, light, and physical structure on community composition.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond the simplistic 'cold-adapted' paradigm to establish a mechanistic framework linking regional geochemistry to microbial selection. It will directly test Liebig's law in a comparative polar context and provide predictive insight into how Arctic freshening and Antarctic iron fertilization may differentially alter ocean biogeochemistry and carbon export.
proposals.novelty: While polar microbiology often focuses on temperature adaptation, this proposal explicitly tests the non-obvious premise that historical nutrient and light regimes—derived from fundamentally different continental configurations and hydrological cycles—imprint a stronger selective signature than temperature alone in structuring microbial function.

proposals.title: Lateral Advection of Particulate Organic Matter as a Driver of Deep-Sea Microbial Community Homogenization Across Latitudes
proposals.motivation: Deep ocean microbial communities show surprising similarity across vast latitudinal distances, challenging the paradigm of isolated, nutrient-limited basins. Pre-2026 work, including in the Gulf of Mexico, has established that lateral transport of organic matter from continental margins is a significant carbon source for the deep sea, but its role in structuring microbial biogeography remains unclear. This research addresses a critical gap in understanding how physical connectivity, rather than just in-situ conditions, shapes deep ocean biodiversity and ecosystem function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the lateral advection of particulate organic matter (POM) from productive continental shelves, mediated by mesoscale eddies and boundary currents, transports both substrates and microbial seed populations, thereby reducing beta-diversity and increasing functional similarity in deep-sea heterotrophic bacterial communities across latitudes.
proposals.method: We will leverage time-series data and samples from established programs in the Northern Gulf of Mexico, a river-dominated shelf with well-studied hypoxia and lateral carbon export. Using a combination of sediment trap deployments along cross-shelf transects and hydrographic surveys to track eddy fields, we will quantify the flux and isotopic (δ13C, δ15N) signature of exported POM. Deep-sea (≥1000m) microbial community composition and functional potential (via 16S rRNA gene amplicon and metagenomic sequencing) will be analyzed and correlated with physical transport metrics and POM chemistry using multivariate statistical models (e.g., Mantel tests, distance-based redundancy analysis) to partition the variance explained by spatial distance versus shared resource provenance.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the 'resource connectivity' hypothesis for deep-sea microbial biogeography, moving beyond descriptions of pattern to identify a specific mechanism. It will integrate Dr. Kirchman's expertise in carbon cycling, hypoxia, and microbial ecology to quantify how human-altered coastal nutrient loads (a la 'Liebig’s Law and Haber’s Tragedy') may indirectly influence deep ocean community assembly and resilience through altered lateral carbon subsidies.
proposals.novelty: While lateral transport is known to supply carbon, its role as a simultaneous vector for microbial dispersal and a homogenizing agent for community function is non-obvious and untested. This proposal uniquely bridges physical oceanography of shelf-deep ocean connectivity with microbial genomic analysis to explain latitudinal similarity.

proposals.title: Spatial Patterning of Microbial Communities in a Hypoxic Coastal Seascape: A Landscape Ecology Approach
proposals.motivation: The spatial distribution of marine microbes is critical for biogeochemical cycles, including the fate of organic matter and nitrogen transformations in hypoxic zones like the Gulf of Mexico. However, microbial 'seascapes' are often analyzed as point measurements, lacking a quantitative framework to describe their spatial structure and connectivity. Applying landscape ecology metrics can bridge this gap, providing a novel way to link microbial patchiness to physical and chemical drivers of hypoxia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that microbial community distributions on the Louisiana-Texas shelf exhibit non-random spatial patterns (e.g., patches, gradients) that correlate with landscape features of the hypoxic zone, and that these patterns differ significantly between oxic and hypoxic waters, reflecting distinct ecological controls.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing microbial community data (16S/18S rRNA gene amplicon sequences) and concurrent hydrographic/nutrient data from Gulf of Mexico summer cruises (pre-2026). Using georeferenced sample stations, we will calculate landscape metrics such as Moran's I for spatial autocorrelation of key taxa and functional groups, and employ FRAGSTATS-style patch analysis on interpolated microbial biomass maps. These spatial metrics will be statistically related to the Hypoxia Index and gradients of river-derived nutrients (N, Si) to identify environmental correlates of microbial patch structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, spatially explicit model of microbial community structure in the Gulf's hypoxic zone, moving beyond composition to pattern. It will establish a new analytical framework ('microbial seascape ecology') for linking microbial biogeography to ecosystem-scale processes like organic matter decomposition and denitrification in dead zones.
proposals.novelty: While landscape ecology is standard in terrestrial studies, its application to pelagic microbial communities is nascent. This proposal uniquely integrates these spatial statistics with Kirchman's expertise in coastal microbial ecology and hypoxia to mechanistically understand how physical water column structure shapes biological communities.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Historical Hypoxia and Nitrogen Cycling in the Gulf of Mexico from Sedimentary Microbial Genomic Fossils
proposals.motivation: Reconstructing past ocean conditions is critical for contextualizing modern climate-driven changes like the expansion of coastal hypoxic zones. The Gulf of Mexico's large, river-dominated shelf experiences severe seasonal hypoxia linked to nutrient loading, but its long-term variability prior to instrumental records is poorly constrained. Sedimentary ancient DNA (sedaDNA) from microbes serves as a direct, biological archive of past environmental stressors, offering a novel proxy to complement geochemical records.
proposals.hypothesis: The taxonomic and functional gene composition of preserved microbial communities in sediment cores accurately encodes the history of bottom-water oxygen concentration and nitrogen nutrient regimes on the Gulf of Mexico shelf. Specifically, we hypothesize that genetic signatures of anaerobic metabolisms and nitrogen transformation pathways will correlate with known periods of hypoxia.
proposals.method: We will analyze sedaDNA from dated sediment cores spanning the last 500 years from the northern Gulf of Mexico hypoxic zone. Shotgun metagenomic sequencing will be performed, followed by taxonomic profiling using MetaPhlAn and functional potential analysis via HUMAnN. A machine learning inverse model (e.g., a Random Forest or Bayesian regression) will be trained on the modern relationship between water column data (oxygen, nutrients) and surface sediment microbial DNA. This trained model will then be applied to down-core sedaDNA profiles to infer historical conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, high-resolution reconstruction of hypoxia and nitrogen cycling in the Gulf of Mexico, extending the record beyond direct measurements. It will directly test the application of sedaDNA as a quantitative paleo-environmental proxy, bridging microbial ecology and paleoceanography. The findings will inform models on the sensitivity of coastal systems to climate and anthropogenic nutrient forcing.
proposals.novelty: While sedaDNA is used for community reconstruction, its application as a quantitative tool to solve the inverse problem of inferring specific past chemical conditions (like oxygen) in marine systems is nascent. This approach moves beyond correlation to develop a calibrated, mechanistic model linking genetic potential to environmental parameters in a key region impacted by human activity.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Relative Contribution of Water Column Redoxclines and Sediments to Nitrogen Loss on the Northern Gulf of Mexico Hypoxic Shelf
proposals.motivation: The large and recurrent hypoxic zone in the northern Gulf of Mexico is a critical ecosystem stressor driven by riverine nitrogen (N) loading. While sediments are known sites of N removal via denitrification and anammox, the quantitative role of water column redoxclines (suboxic zones) in pelagic N loss remains poorly constrained. Determining the dominant pathway is essential for accurate N budgets and for predicting the efficacy of nutrient management strategies aimed at reducing hypoxia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that on the seasonally hypoxic Louisiana-Texas shelf, sediments are the dominant sink for fixed nitrogen, and that the contribution of water column redoxclines, while spatially variable, is secondary outside of intense, mid-water hypoxia.
proposals.method: We will conduct seasonal cruises across the shelf, targeting stations spanning oxic, hypoxic, and anoxic conditions. Concurrent measurements of N removal rates will be made using isotope pairing techniques (15N-NO3- and 15N-NH4+) on intact sediment cores and in water column samples incubated across redox gradients. High-resolution vertical profiles of O2, nutrients, and N2/Ar ratios (via membrane inlet mass spectrometry) will identify redoxcline boundaries and integrate pelagic N2 production. Rate measurements will be scaled using geostatistical kriging and GIS-based analyses of sediment and water column zones defined by our hydrographic data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first spatially and temporally integrated budget partitioning N removal between sediments and the water column for this system. It will directly test the 'sediment focus' paradigm in a major river-dominated shelf sea. The results will refine models of coastal N cycling and provide a mechanistic basis for evaluating how shifting hypoxia patterns may alter the shelf's nitrogen filter.
proposals.novelty: Previous studies have measured these processes in isolation; our novel synthesis concurrently quantifies both pathways in the same water masses and sediments, allowing a direct, system-scale comparison. This approach moves beyond cataloging potential rates to identifying the dominant environmental controllers of the actual N sink strength.

proposals.title: Reconstructing a Century of Mississippi River Nitrogen Loading Using a Process-Informed Inverse Model of Shelf Sediment Archives
proposals.motivation: The development of seasonal hypoxia in the northern Gulf of Mexico is directly linked to anthropogenic nitrogen (N) loads from the Mississippi-Atchafalaya River Basin, but the long-term historical trajectory of these loads remains uncertain. Pre-2026 water quality monitoring is temporally limited, hindering our ability to separate climate-driven variability from human-driven trends and to validate watershed model hindcasts. This gap constrains predictions of hypoxia response to future nutrient management and climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the history of riverine nitrogen delivery is encoded in the concentration, isotopic composition (δ¹⁵N), and accumulation rates of organic matter and bioavailable N forms within dated sediment cores from the Louisiana-Texas shelf. A process-based inverse model can decode this sedimentary archive to reconstruct decadal-scale N loads.
proposals.method: We will analyze a suite of existing sediment cores from across the shelf's hypoxic zone for down-core profiles of total organic nitrogen, δ¹⁵N of organic matter, and biogenic silica (as a diatom productivity proxy). These data will be integrated into a Bayesian inverse model framework. The forward model will couple a simplified sediment diagenesis module (representing burial and isotope fractionation) with a regional shelf productivity response function to N loading. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling will be used to infer the most probable history of N inputs that explains the observed sedimentary signatures, constrained by known watershed fertilizer use and river discharge data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, independent reconstruction of 20th-century N loading to the northern Gulf, providing critical validation for watershed models. It will quantify the relative roles of agricultural intensification (Haber's legacy) and climate variability in driving hypoxia development, directly addressing themes in Kirchman's recent work. The inverse framework will be a new tool for extracting land-use history from sedimentary records in other river-dominated systems.
proposals.novelty: While sediment cores have been used as qualitative records of eutrophication, this study innovates by formally framing it as an inverse problem to quantitatively reconstruct N loads. The explicit integration of a process-based sediment diagenesis model within a Bayesian inference scheme to 'deconvolve' the loading signal from post-depositional alteration is a novel quantitative advance beyond simple correlation.

proposals.title: Contrasting Nitrogen Cycling and Oxygen Demand in Sediments of the Northern Gulf of Mexico: A Comparative Study of Pristine and Eutrophic Shelf Zones
proposals.motivation: Coastal hypoxia, driven by riverine nutrient loading, is a persistent issue on river-dominated shelves like the northern Gulf of Mexico. While the broad link between nitrogen (N) inputs and hypoxia is established, the specific microbial and geochemical processes in sediments that control oxygen consumption and N removal (e.g., denitrification) are poorly quantified across a gradient of N loading. Understanding how these sediment processes differ between low-N (pristine) and high-N (eutrophic) zones is critical for predicting system response to nutrient management and for refining models of coastal hypoxia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sediments under high-N loading will exhibit fundamentally different N-cycling pathways and efficiencies, with a higher proportion of organic matter mineralization coupled to nitrate reduction (denitrification and DNRA) rather than aerobic respiration, but that the efficiency of N removal via denitrification will be saturated or reduced due to oxygen stress, leading to greater net oxygen demand and ammonium regeneration.
proposals.method: We will conduct seasonal cruises to sample sediment cores from paired sites on the Louisiana-Texas shelf: a chronically hypoxic, high-N zone influenced by the Mississippi-Atchafalaya River System and a seasonally normoxic, low-N control zone (e.g., western Texas shelf). Core incubations will measure process rates (aerobic respiration, denitrification via isotope pairing, anammox, DNRA, nitrification) using 15N tracer techniques and microsensor O2/N2O profiles. Porewater chemistry (NO3-, NH4+, Fe2+, Mn2+, SO42-) will be analyzed. Microbial community structure for N-cycling genes (nirS, nirK, nosZ, amoA, hzo) will be characterized via qPCR and amplicon sequencing. Data will be integrated using multivariate statistics and used to parameterize a reactive-transport model for each site type.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, process-based comparison of sediment biogeochemistry across a major nutrient gradient. It will directly quantify how eutrophication alters the partitioning of carbon oxidation pathways and the net sediment oxygen demand, and will test whether the 'dead zone' sediments become a less efficient sink for reactive nitrogen. The results will deliver critical empirical rates for biogeochemical models predicting hypoxia extent and nutrient legacy effects.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies characterize water-column hypoxia or single-site sediment processes, this paired-site, multi-process comparison across a pristine-eutrophic continuum on the same shelf is novel. It directly tests Liebig's law and stoichiometric controls in sediments by comparing systems limited by organic carbon vs. nitrate, linking microbial genetic potential to in situ process measurements under realistic environmental gradients.

proposals.title: Constraining the Global Significance of DMSO Reduction as a Source of Dimethyl Sulfide in Contrasting Coastal and Shelf Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Dimethyl sulfide (DMS) is a climatically important biogenic gas, yet its marine sources remain incompletely quantified. The reduction of dimethylsulfoniopropionate (DMSP) is well-studied, but the role of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) reduction is poorly constrained, with some studies suggesting it is a minor pathway. This knowledge gap limits our ability to model DMS production, especially under the variable nutrient and oxygen regimes characteristic of large river-influenced shelves like the Gulf of Mexico, where hypoxia is a persistent feature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that DMSO reduction is an insignificant source of DMS on a global scale, contributing minimally compared to DMSP cleavage, particularly in hypoxic, nutrient-rich coastal zones where alternative microbial sulfur cycling pathways dominate.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard incubation experiments in the northern Gulf of Mexico, targeting the river-dominated shelf and the perennial hypoxic zone. Using stable isotope tracer techniques (35S-DMSO and 35S-DMSP), we will directly measure DMSO and DMSP reduction rates alongside concurrent measurements of nutrient profiles, dissolved oxygen, and microbial community structure (via 16S rRNA gene sequencing). Rates will be integrated across defined biogeochemical zones (e.g., oxic surface, hypoxic bottom layer, river plume front) to calculate areal and volumetric fluxes, which will be scaled using existing global data syntheses on similar shelf systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rate measurements of DMSO reduction in a major hypoxic coastal system, directly testing its quantitative importance. It will refine global DMS budget models by constraining a key uncertain flux. Furthermore, it will link DMS production pathways to the nitrogen and oxygen dynamics central to Dr. Kirchman's recent research on hypoxia and nutrient cycling.
proposals.novelty: While DMSO reduction has been examined in open-ocean or estuarine settings, its significance has not been systematically tested in the context of large-scale seasonal hypoxia and strong terrestrial nutrient gradients, where redox conditions and microbial metabolism are fundamentally altered.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity and Bioavailability of the Dissolved DMSO Pool in a River-Dominated Coastal Hypoxic Zone
proposals.motivation: Dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) is a major reservoir of organic sulfur and a potential oxygen sink in marine systems, yet its cycling in coastal hypoxic zones is poorly understood. In the context of expanding dead zones like the northern Gulf of Mexico, understanding the bioavailability of organic sulfur compounds like DMSO is critical for modeling microbial metabolism, sulfur cycling, and oxygen demand under hypoxia. Pre-2026 work has largely treated dissolved DMSO as a single, labile pool, ignoring potential refractory components that could persist and influence long-term biogeochemical dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: The dissolved DMSO pool in coastal hypoxic waters is heterogeneous, containing a significant refractory component that is not rapidly bioavailable to the ambient microbial community, and its bioavailability varies spatially with oxygen concentration and nutrient regime.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard bioassay experiments on water collected from distinct zones (oxic surface, hypoxic bottom, river plume) on the Louisiana-Texas shelf. Dissolved DMSO concentration will be tracked over time via purge-and-trap gas chromatography. We will use a dual-tracer approach: 1) measuring the drawdown of bulk DMSO, and 2) tracking the incorporation of a 35S-DMSO tracer into microbial biomass and into the dissolved DMS/DMSP pools. Bioavailability will be quantified using first-order uptake rate constants, and the refractory fraction will be defined as the non-consumed pool after extended incubation. Statistical analysis (ANOVA, regression) will relate DMSO cycling rates to environmental parameters (O2, nutrients, microbial community structure via 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct quantification of the refractory fraction of dissolved DMSO in a coastal ocean setting, challenging the paradigm of its uniform lability. It will directly link DMSO bioavailability to hypoxia dynamics, offering a new mechanistic understanding of organic sulfur cycling and its role in oxygen consumption on nutrient-impacted shelves. The results will refine biogeochemical models of coastal hypoxia by incorporating realistic, multi-component DMSO dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While DMSO production and consumption have been studied, the potential heterogeneity and refractory nature of the dissolved pool in dynamic coastal systems is unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates the concept of organic matter lability from carbon/nitrogen cycling (a Kirchman lab theme) into the sulfur cycle within the explicit context of a well-studied hypoxic zone.

proposals.title: Linking Microbial DMSO Dissimilation to Carbon Cycling and Hypoxia on the Northern Gulf of Mexico Shelf
proposals.motivation: Dissolved dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) is a major organic sulfur reservoir in marine systems, yet its microbial dissimilation to CO2 remains a poorly quantified component of carbon cycling. On river-dominated shelves like the Northern Gulf of Mexico, where seasonal hypoxia is driven by organic matter degradation, understanding alternative respiratory pathways like DMSO reduction is critical for accurately modeling oxygen demand and carbon fate in the 'dead zone'.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, previously uncultivated bacteria in Gulf of Mexico shelf sediments and hypoxic bottom waters are responsible for the majority of DMSO dissimilation, and that this process contributes significantly to community respiration and CO2 production, especially under low-oxygen conditions.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard incubations with 13C-DMSO across the shelf's oxic-hypoxic gradient, tracking DMSO loss and 13CO2 production. Active DMSO-assimilating and dissimilating taxa will be identified via DNA/RNA Stable Isotope Probing (SIP) followed by 16S rRNA gene amplicon and metagenomic sequencing of heavy fractions. Key genes (e.g., DMSO reductase, dmsA) will be phylogenetically anchored. Enrichment cultures from SIP inocula will be established under varying oxygen and nutrient (N, S) regimes to isolate dominant DMSO respirers, linking physiology to in situ biogeochemical zones.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify the key microbial players and genetic markers for DMSO dissimilation, directly quantifying its contribution to carbon mineralization. It will provide a mechanistic link between sulfur and carbon cycles in a major coastal hypoxic system, refining models of oxygen consumption by incorporating this overlooked respiratory pathway.
proposals.novelty: While DMSO cycling is studied, the specific taxa responsible for its complete dissimilation to CO2 in complex coastal systems are unknown. This proposal uniquely integrates SIP, cultivation, and biogeochemical zonation to connect identity, function, and ecosystem impact, applying Kirchman's expertise in Gulf hypoxia and nutrient dynamics to a novel carbon flow.

proposals.title: Enzymatic Pathways for DMSO Respiration in Hypoxic Coastal Sediments: Linking Sulfur Cycling to Nitrogen and Oxygen Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Dissolved dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) is a major reservoir of organic sulfur in marine systems and a potential terminal electron acceptor for microbial respiration, particularly in hypoxic zones like the Gulf of Mexico. Understanding its biochemical utilization is critical for modeling sulfur cycling and its coupling to nitrogen and carbon cycles in oxygen-depleted environments. This directly addresses the 'dead zone' dynamics central to Kirchman's work, where alternative respiratory pathways can influence hypoxia persistence and nutrient regeneration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, phylogenetically conserved enzymatic systems for DMSO respiration are active in sediments of the hypoxic Gulf of Mexico, and that their expression and activity are regulated by gradients of oxygen, nitrate, and organic matter.
proposals.method: We will analyze sediment cores from established transects across the Louisiana-Texas shelf hypoxia zone. Metagenomic and metatranscriptomic sequencing (Illumina NovaSeq) will identify genes and transcripts for known (e.g., DmsAB-type) and novel DMSO reductase systems.
