Activity will be quantified via 35S-DMSO tracer assays in slurry incubations under manipulated oxygen and nitrate conditions. Bioinformatic analysis will involve co-assembly (MEGAHIT), gene annotation (DRAM, KEGG), and mapping to curated DMSO reduction protein families to link phylogenetic identity (via 16S rRNA genes) to functional potential.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct link between genetic potential, expression, and activity of DMSO respiration enzymes in a major coastal hypoxic system. It will quantify the significance of DMSO as an electron acceptor relative to nitrate and oxygen, refining biogeochemical models of the Gulf dead zone. The results will advance our mechanistic understanding of microbial metabolic plasticity under hypoxia.
proposals.novelty: While DMSO reduction is studied in isolated cultures, its in-situ enzymatic pathways and ecological drivers in complex, nutrient-rich, hypoxic sediments are unknown. This proposal uniquely integrates molecular 'omics' with process measurements and nutrient gradient analysis, a powerful approach from Kirchman's recent work, to resolve a key but overlooked component of shelf redox chemistry.

proposals.title: Partitioning and Fate of Dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) in a River-Dominated Coastal Hypoxic Zone: A Dual Tracer Study of Particulate and Dissolved Pools
proposals.motivation: Dimethylsulfoniopropionate (DMSP) and its oxidation product dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) are key components of the marine sulfur cycle, influencing climate via aerosol formation. In hypoxic zones like the northern Gulf of Mexico, microbial transformations of these compounds are poorly constrained, particularly the role of particle-associated versus free-living communities. Understanding the partitioning and fate of DMSO is critical for modeling sulfur dynamics under the dual stressors of eutrophication and hypoxia, which are driven by riverine nutrient loads as highlighted in Kirchman's prior work on hypoxia indices and Liebig's Law.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that particulate DMSO, derived from phytoplankton and particle-attached bacteria, will have significantly higher microbial transformation rates and a shorter residence time than dissolved DMSO in the hypoxic water column, acting as a dominant sink and source of dimethyl sulfide (DMS) in this system.
proposals.method: The study will be conducted on the Louisiana-Texas shelf during summer hypoxia. Water column profiles will be sampled across oxic, hypoxic, and anoxic zones. Particulate and dissolved DMSO will be separated via sequential filtration (GF/F for particulate, 0.2-µm for dissolved). Transformation rates (production and consumption) for each pool will be quantified using stable isotope tracer incubations with 13C- or 34S-labeled DMSO, followed by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) analysis. Concurrent measurements of nutrients, chlorophyll-a, bacterial production (via 3H-leucine incorporation), and community structure (16S rRNA gene sequencing) will contextualize the process rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative assessment of the dual DMSO pools in a major hypoxic region, directly linking sulfur cycling to microbial community dynamics and redox conditions. It will refine budgets for climatically active DMS by identifying the dominant microbial pathways and environmental controls on its precursor, DMSO, within a highly dynamic coastal system.
proposals.novelty: While DMSO dynamics have been studied in open oceans, the explicit comparison of particulate versus dissolved DMSO transformation rates and their modulation by hypoxia is novel, integrating Kirchman's expertise in coastal microbial ecology and nutrient-driven hypoxia.

proposals.title: Mesocosm Experiments Revealing the Interactive Effects of Jellyfish Blooms and Nutrient Loading on Hypoxia Dynamics in a River-Dominated Shelf System
proposals.motivation: Recurrent hypoxia in systems like the northern Gulf of Mexico is a critical threat to marine ecosystems and fisheries. While nutrient loading from rivers is the primary driver, the potential role of gelatinous zooplankton (jellyfish) in modulating hypoxia through their biogeochemical impacts is poorly understood. Isolating the causal links between these factors in the field is challenging due to confounding physical and biological variability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jellyfish presence will exacerbate hypoxia under high nutrient conditions by enhancing microbial respiration through the release of labile organic matter, but may mitigate hypoxia under lower nutrient loads by sequestering nitrogen into biomass and reducing phytoplankton-derived organic matter.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale, flow-through mesocosm tanks to simulate a river-influenced coastal shelf environment. Treatments will cross two factors: nutrient levels (ambient vs. elevated nitrate+phosphate, mimicking riverine input) and biological community (with vs. without the common scyphozoan *Aurelia aurita*). We will monitor dissolved oxygen, inorganic nutrients (N, P), particulate organic carbon/nitrogen, and bacterial production over diel cycles. The Hypoxia Index (HI) methodology from Kirchman's prior work will be applied to quantify hypoxia severity in each tank. Statistical analysis will use factorial ANOVA to test for main and interactive effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental quantification of how jellyfish interact with nutrient loading to control coastal hypoxia. It will advance the conceptual model of hypoxia formation beyond Liebig's law of the minimum by incorporating top-down consumer effects on nutrient cycling and oxygen demand. The results will inform predictive models and management strategies for ecosystems experiencing concurrent eutrophication and shifts in food-web structure.
proposals.novelty: While mesocosms are common in limnology, their application to isolate jellyfish-nutrient interactions in a realistic coastal shelf context is novel. This approach moves beyond correlative field studies to establish mechanistic, causal links between these co-occurring drivers of hypoxia.

proposals.title: Network Analysis of Hypoxia Drivers in a Large River-Dominated Shelf: Quantifying Interactions Between Physical Forcing, Nutrient Loading, and Microbial Metabolism
proposals.motivation: Hypoxia in coastal zones like the northern Gulf of Mexico is a critical threat to marine ecosystems and fisheries, driven by complex interactions between climate, riverine nutrient loads, and microbial processes. Traditional correlative and index-based approaches (e.g., Hypoxia Index) struggle to quantify the strength and directionality of feedbacks between these drivers, limiting predictive capacity and management strategies. A systems-level framework is needed to move beyond identifying individual limiting factors (Liebig's Law) to understanding the network of interactions that sustain low-oxygen conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that network analysis will reveal that hypoxia persistence is governed not by a single dominant driver but by a set of strong, non-linear feedback loops, particularly between physical stratification, nitrogen remineralization pathways, and microbial carbon demand, which stabilize the system in a hypoxic state.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-layer network model for the Louisiana-Texas shelf using pre-2026 observational and model-derived data (physical, chemical, biological). Nodes will represent key parameters (e.g., river discharge, nitrate load, stratification strength, chlorophyll a, bacterial production, oxygen concentration). Edges will be quantified using convergent cross-mapping (CCM) and partial correlation to infer causal directionality and interaction strength. Network metrics (e.g., centrality, modularity, path analysis) will be calculated using the 'igraph' package in R to identify critical control points and feedback structures within the system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantified interaction network for a major hypoxic zone, explicitly mapping the causal web linking human activity (nutrient loading) to climate forcing and microbial ecosystem function. It will advance the field from statistical description to a mechanistic, systems-level understanding, identifying key leverage points for intervention. The network framework will serve as a testable template for other river-dominated shelves globally.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Kirchman's prior work has identified key variables and indices for hypoxia, applying formal network analysis from systems ecology to this problem is novel. It moves beyond identifying co-varying 'zones' to dynamically modeling the directional interactions and feedbacks between 'dead' zone formation, nutrient cycles, and microbial processes, offering a new paradigm for hypoxia research.

proposals.title: Bacterial Community Modulation as a Top-Down Control on Jellyfish Blooms in a Hypoxic, River-Dominated Shelf System
proposals.motivation: In systems like the northern Gulf of Mexico, hypoxia is a well-studied consequence of eutrophication and phytoplankton blooms. Jellyfish blooms are often considered a problematic response to these altered conditions, outcompeting fish and disrupting food webs. This proposal flips the paradigm, asking if targeted manipulation of the microbial loop—specifically the bacteria that process organic matter from dying blooms—could suppress the formation of jellyfish blooms by altering the basal resource pool.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific bacterial functional groups, by rapidly remineralizing labile organic matter from phytoplankton, can outcompete gelatinous zooplankton for essential nutrients (e.g., nitrogen), thereby limiting the initiation and magnitude of subsequent jellyfish blooms.
proposals.method: We will conduct seasonal mesocosm experiments in the northern Gulf of Mexico, manipulating bacterial communities with specific carbon substrates and inhibitors (e.g., prokaryotic production inhibitors vs. eukaryotic inhibitors) following simulated phytoplankton bloom senescence. We will quantify changes in bacterial community structure via 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing and track nutrient (N, P) partitioning using stable isotope (15N) tracers. Jellyfish polyp settlement, strobilation, and medusae production will be monitored alongside key water column parameters (oxygen, nutrients, POM). Statistical modeling will use structural equation modeling (SEM) to disentangle direct and indirect microbial drivers on jellyfish success.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel mechanistic link between microbial community function and gelatinous zooplankton population dynamics. It will contribute a new conceptual framework for ecosystem management, suggesting that 'managing the microbes' could be a leverage point for mitigating higher-trophic-level regime shifts. The results will directly inform coupled biogeochemical-ecological models of hypoxia-prone shelves.
proposals.novelty: While bacterial roles in carbon cycling are well-established, their potential as a direct, manageable control point for suppressing jellyfish blooms is unexplored. This approach reverses the typical driver-response narrative of hypoxia research, treating the bacterial community not just as a responder but as an active controller of ecosystem state.

proposals.title: Dispersal Limitation and High Turnover of Rare Microbial Taxa in a Seasonally Hypoxic Coastal System
proposals.motivation: The 'everything is everywhere' hypothesis posits that rare microbial taxa are cosmopolitan with high dispersal and low loss, but this remains largely untested in dynamic coastal systems where physical and chemical gradients are strong. The northern Gulf of Mexico shelf, with its pronounced seasonal hypoxia driven by riverine nutrient inputs, provides an ideal natural laboratory to test this paradigm, as dispersal and loss are influenced by water column stratification, currents, and oxygen stress.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, contrary to the cosmopolitan hypothesis, rare microbial taxa in the northern Gulf of Mexico exhibit high biogeographic structuring (indicating dispersal limitation) and experience high rates of local extinction (loss) in response to seasonal hypoxia and other environmental perturbations.
proposals.method: We will analyze a multi-year, high-resolution 16S rRNA gene amplicon dataset from the Louisiana-Texas shelf, partitioned into distinct biogeochemical zones (e.g., river plume, hypoxic zone, offshore). Using established bioinformatics pipelines (QIIME2, DADA2), we will define rare biosphere operational taxonomic units (OTUs) with low relative abundance. We will apply neutral community models (NCM) to quantify the deviation from neutral dispersal, and calculate temporal turnover rates (e.g., Bray-Curtis dissimilarity, extinction estimates) for rare taxa across seasons and hypoxia development. Co-occurrence network analysis will assess if rare taxa are loosely connected, supporting high loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a core tenet of microbial biogeography in a critical coastal ecosystem, linking rare biosphere dynamics to anthropogenic eutrophication and hypoxia. It will provide a mechanistic framework for understanding how climate and human-driven variability, as explored in Kirchman's prior work, filters microbial communities through dispersal and loss processes.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the classic assumption by explicitly testing for dispersal limitation and high loss of rare taxa, rather than assuming their ubiquity and persistence, within the context of a well-defined and human-impacted physical-chemical gradient.

proposals.title: Stasis in the Rare Biosphere: Testing the Temporal Persistence of Microbial Biogeography in a Seasonally Hypoxic Coastal System
proposals.motivation: The 'rare biosphere' is a vast reservoir of microbial diversity, but its ecological role and temporal dynamics are poorly understood. Prevailing models suggest rare taxa are transient 'seed bank' members, contributing to high temporal turnover. This proposal challenges that view, asking if rare microbial biogeography is stable, which would imply a more structured and persistent functional role in ecosystems like the hypoxic northern Gulf of Mexico.
proposals.hypothesis: The biogeographic patterns of rare bacterial and archaeal taxa in the northern Gulf of Mexico are not transient but are stable over seasonal and interannual timescales, exhibiting predictable spatial structuring linked to persistent physicochemical gradients (e.g., oxygen, nutrients).
proposals.method: We will analyze a multi-year (pre-2026) time-series of 16S rRNA gene amplicon data from the Louisiana-Texas shelf, focusing on the hypoxic zone. Rare taxa will be defined as those with <0.1% relative abundance in any sample. Temporal stability will be assessed using the Raup-Crick metric on presence/absence data to quantify turnover against a null model of random assembly. Spatial persistence will be mapped using multivariate statistics (PERMANOVA, Mantel tests) to correlate community dissimilarity with static environmental fields (e.g., long-term mean nutrient concentrations, bottom topography). Co-occurrence network analysis (e.g., SPIEC-EASI) will identify stable, non-random associations among rare taxa.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous test of the 'transient vs. stable' paradigm for the rare biosphere, directly linking microbial biogeography to the long-studied physical and chemical gradients of the Gulf's dead zone. It will advance our understanding of whether rare microbes represent a persistent, resilient component of coastal ecosystems or merely stochastic noise.
proposals.novelty: While microbial biogeography often focuses on abundant taxa or snapshot studies, this work applies rigorous null models and time-series analysis from a well-characterized system specifically to test the stability of rare taxa, integrating Kirchman's expertise in coastal carbon/nitrogen cycles with contemporary bioinformatics.

proposals.title: The Biogeography and Biogeochemical Role of Hyper-Abundant Phylotypes in the Hypoxic Northern Gulf of Mexico
proposals.motivation: The 'rare biosphere' has dominated microbial ecology, but the ecological role of the most abundant phylotypes, which dominate microbial biomass and activity, is poorly understood in dynamic coastal systems. In the seasonally hypoxic northern Gulf of Mexico, these 'hyper-abundant' organisms are likely key drivers of carbon remineralization and nitrogen transformations, directly influencing the formation and persistence of the 'dead zone'. Understanding their distribution and function is critical for predicting hypoxia responses to nutrient loading and climate variability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a small number of hyper-abundant bacterial and archaeal phylotypes exhibit predictable biogeography across the river-ocean gradient of the northern Gulf of Mexico, and that their relative abundance and activity are primary controls on the spatial extent and intensity of summer hypoxia through their roles in organic matter degradation and nitrogen cycling.
proposals.method: We will analyze a multi-year, spatially-resolved 16S rRNA gene amplicon dataset from the Louisiana-Texas shelf, applying rank-abundance curves and a 1% relative abundance threshold to define 'hyper-abundant' phylotypes. Their biogeography will be mapped against hydrographic zones (river plume, frontal, hypoxic, offshore) and correlated with concurrent measurements of dissolved oxygen, nutrients (N, P, Si), and organic carbon using multivariate statistics (RDA, Mantel tests). We will use PICRUSt2 to infer metagenomic functional potential, focusing on genes for aerobic respiration, denitrification, and sulfate reduction to link abundance to hypoxia-relevant biogeochemistry.
proposals.contribution: This work will shift focus from microbial diversity to microbial dominance, explicitly linking the distribution of the most abundant taxa to hypoxia dynamics. It will produce a quantitative framework for identifying key microbial players in coastal eutrophication and test whether Liebig's law of the minimum applies to the community structure of these dominant organisms across nutrient gradients.
proposals.novelty: While prior work characterizes the microbial community in hypoxia, this study uniquely targets the biogeography and inferred function of the most extremely abundant phylotypes, treating them as a distinct functional entity to explain large-scale biogeochemical patterns, moving beyond community-wide correlations.

proposals.title: Functional Integration of Horizontally Acquired Genes Determines Niche Partitioning of Coastal Bacterioplankton Across a Hypoxia-Induced DOM Gradient
proposals.motivation: In river-dominated shelves like the northern Gulf of Mexico, seasonal hypoxia alters the composition and bioavailability of dissolved organic matter (DOM), creating distinct microbial niches. While horizontal gene transfer (HGT) is widespread, its causal role in shaping these niches and ecosystem function, particularly in response to anthropogenic nutrient loading and climate-driven hypoxia, remains correlative and untested. This limits our ability to predict microbial community responses to changing coastal conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the functional integration and expression of horizontally acquired genes for DOM utilization, not their mere presence, are the primary determinants of bacterial niche boundaries across chemical gradients characteristic of hypoxic zones.
proposals.method: We will use genetically tractable, ecologically relevant gammaproteobacterial isolates from Gulf of Mexico time-series as recipients for conjugative transfer of putative DOM utilization operons identified via pangenomic analysis of natural populations. Recipient fitness and gene expression (via RNA-seq) will be quantified in chemostat competition experiments under defined DOM regimes mimicking pre-hypoxic and hypoxic conditions. Metabolic modeling (using flux balance analysis with tools like COBRApy) will integrate genomic and transcriptomic data to predict niche outcomes, which will be validated experimentally.
proposals.contribution: This work will move microbial ecology from correlative patterns to mechanistic, causal understanding of how HGT drives niche adaptation. It will directly quantify the fitness value of specific gene acquisitions in an ecosystem-relevant context, linking genetic change to biogeochemical function. The results will refine models of microbial community assembly under the dual stressors of eutrophication and hypoxia.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct experimental manipulation of HGT events within an environmental context to disentangle genetic acquisition from functional integration, a step beyond observational 'omics. This causal framework is rarely applied to complex, multifactorial processes like DOM cycling in coastal oceans.

proposals.title: Continuous Supply Overcomes Sequential Depletion: Testing the Hierarchy of Marine Bacterial DOM Preferences in Chemostat Culture
proposals.motivation: Understanding bacterial dissolved organic matter (DOM) utilization is critical for predicting carbon cycling and oxygen dynamics in systems like the Gulf of Mexico, where riverine inputs create complex DOM mixtures and hypoxia. Batch experiments, the dominant method, confound true substrate preference with the artifact of sequential concentration depletion, failing to mimic the continuous substrate supply of many natural environments. This limits our ability to apply concepts like Liebig's law or metabolic theory to in situ carbon fluxes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when DOM substrates are maintained at constant, non-limiting concentrations, marine heterotrophic bacteria will exhibit simultaneous, flexible co-metabolism of chemically distinct compounds, rather than a rigid, hierarchical preference sequence observed in batch cultures.
proposals.method: We will employ dual-substrate chemostat continuous cultures using natural bacterial assemblages from the Gulf of Mexico's hypoxic zone. Two chemically distinct, isotopically labeled DOM substrates (e.g., a carbohydrate and an amino acid mixture) will be supplied at constant, non-limiting concentrations via precision pumps. Real-time uptake kinetics for each substrate will be quantified using continuous-flow isotope ratio mass spectrometry (CF-IRMS) of evolved CO2, while biomass production and community composition will be tracked via flow cytometry and 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing. System parameters (dilution rate, substrate ratios) will be varied to test for concentration-dependent metabolic shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, mechanistic test of DOM preference hierarchies under environmentally relevant steady-state conditions. It will decouple metabolic regulation from depletion artifacts, offering a new framework for modeling mixed substrate use in biogeochemical models. The results will refine our understanding of carbon processing efficiency in river-dominated shelves and its link to hypoxia development.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the chemostat approach, common in microbial physiology, to the complex problem of natural DOM mixture utilization by diverse assemblages, directly challenging the paradigm inferred from batch culture studies. This isolates the 'adversarial' effect of depletion from true substrate preference.

proposals.title: Resource Choice and Metabolic Switching in Coastal Bacterioplankton: A Dual-Isotope Pulse-Chase Test of DOM Utilization in the Northern Gulf of Mexico
proposals.motivation: In river-dominated shelves like the Northern Gulf of Mexico, seasonal hypoxia is fueled by microbial respiration of complex, mixed dissolved organic matter (DOM) pools from terrestrial and algal sources. Pre-2026 studies, including Kirchman's work on hypoxia indices and nutrient limitation, highlight the need to understand how bacterial substrate preferences control carbon fate and oxygen demand, but traditional single-substrate assays fail to capture realistic resource competition and co-metabolism.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that coastal bacterioplankton communities will exhibit clear preferential utilization of labile, algal-derived DOM over terrestrial DOM when both are available, but that this preference will shift (metabolic switching) as the labile pool is depleted, with co-metabolism influencing the degradation kinetics of the more recalcitrant source.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard incubations with water from the Louisiana-Texas shelf's hypoxic zone. Natural bacterial communities will be simultaneously presented with two isotopically distinct DOM sources: ¹³C-labeled phytoplankton exudates (algal) and ²H-labeled leachate from coastal marsh plants (terrestrial). Using a time-series pulse-chase design, we will track label incorporation into bacterial biomass via phospholipid fatty acid-stable isotope probing (PLFA-SIP) and into respired CO₂. Isotope mixing models and kinetic modeling will quantify preferential uptake, partitioning to respiration versus growth, and the timing of any metabolic switch.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly quantify DOM source preferences and metabolic pathways under mixed-resource conditions, providing a mechanistic link between terrestrial/marine carbon subsidies and microbial respiration rates that drive hypoxia. It will move the field beyond bulk DOC measurements and single-substrate tests to a predictive framework for carbon cycling in anthropogenically impacted coastal zones.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the dual-isotope, resource-choice experimental framework from terrestrial plant ecology to aquatic microbial ecology, allowing simultaneous, direct tracing of multiple DOM fates in a complex natural assemblage, which is a significant advance over sequential or single-substrate SIP studies common pre-2026.

proposals.title: Functional network mapping of dissolved organic matter degradation across microbial phylogeny in the northern Gulf of Mexico hypoxic zone
proposals.motivation: The fate of riverine dissolved organic matter (DOM) on continental shelves like the northern Gulf of Mexico is a critical biogeochemical process influencing carbon cycling and the development of hypoxia. While microbial communities drive DOM degradation, the specific phylogenetic groups responsible for degrading different DOM components and how their activities are coordinated to achieve complete mineralization remain poorly resolved in situ. Understanding this functional network is essential for predicting carbon fluxes and oxygen demand under changing nutrient loads and climate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the complete degradation of complex riverine DOM in the Gulf's hypoxic zone is achieved through a phylogenetically partitioned functional network, where specialized initial degraders (e.g., Bacteroidetes, Planctomycetes) target specific labile compounds, and subsequent mineralizers (e.g., SAR11, Gammaproteobacteria) consume refractory intermediates, with cross-feeding interactions linking these metabolic niches.
proposals.method: We will employ an expanded MICRO-FISH (Microfluidic Isotope Labeling and Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization) platform. Environmental samples from across the hypoxic zone will be incubated with a complex, isotopically labeled DOM cocktail derived from Mississippi River water, containing 13C/15N-labeled amino acids, carbohydrates, and humic-like compounds. We will use a suite of newly designed FISH probes targeting key bacterial groups (Bacteroidetes, Planctomycetes, SAR11, Gammaproteobacteria) to phylogenetically identify individual cells actively incorporating each DOM component, quantified via nanoSIMS. Uptake profiles will be analyzed using network inference algorithms (e.g., SPIEC-EASI) to map consumer-substrate links and potential cross-feeding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first in situ, high-resolution map linking microbial phylogeny to specific DOM component degradation in a major coastal hypoxic system. It will identify keystone taxa for DOM processing stages and reveal metabolic interdependencies. This directly addresses the microbial mechanisms behind the 'Haber's Tragedy' by linking DOM fate to the oxygen demand driving hypoxia, offering a predictive framework for carbon turnover.
proposals.novelty: While MICRO-FISH has tracked single substrates, simultaneously mapping the uptake of numerous DOM compounds across a broad phylogeny within a single sample is novel. This moves beyond identifying 'who is there' or bulk activity to reveal the functional network architecture of complete DOM degradation in a complex natural community.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Controls on Dissolved Organic Matter Utilization Across Coastal Hypoxic and Open Ocean Systems
proposals.motivation: Dissolved organic matter (DOM) is a major carbon reservoir, and its microbial consumption influences carbon sequestration and oxygen demand, particularly in hypoxic zones like the Gulf of Mexico. Pre-2026 work shows hypoxia is driven by nutrient loading and microbial respiration, but the specific phylogenetic groups responsible for DOM turnover in different environments remain poorly quantified. This limits our ability to predict how climate and land-use changes will alter carbon cycling and hypoxia severity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the phylogenetic identity and metabolic strategies of DOM-consuming bacteria differ fundamentally between river-dominated coastal hypoxic zones and the oligotrophic open ocean, driven by DOM composition and nutrient (N) availability as per Liebig's law.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard incubation experiments using stable isotope-labeled (13C, 15N) DOM substrates (e.g., amino acids, carbohydrates, riverine humics) across a gradient from the Mississippi River plume to the Gulf of Mexico's open shelf. Microbial uptake will be linked to phylogenetic identity via NanoSIMS combined with fluorescence in situ hybridization (NanoSIMS-FISH) and 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing. Taxon-specific uptake rates will be calculated using algorithms like iRep to infer activity from sequence data, and correlated with ambient nutrient concentrations and hypoxia metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, taxon-specific rates of DOM utilization across a coastal hypoxia gradient, directly linking microbial community composition to carbon turnover. It will refine models of hypoxia formation by identifying key microbial players and their resource constraints, contributing to better predictions of shelf ecosystem responses to nutrient management and climate variability.
proposals.novelty: While DOM cycling is well-studied, directly measuring phylogenetic group-specific DOM uptake rates in situ across environmental gradients, and explicitly connecting this to hypoxia dynamics, is a novel synthesis of microbial ecology and biogeochemistry. The application of NanoSIMS-FISH to a large-scale shelf hypoxia system is a significant technical advance.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic mapping of dissolved organic matter assimilation in the Northern Gulf of Mexico hypoxic zone using FISH-NanoSIMS
proposals.motivation: The microbial processing of riverine dissolved organic matter (DOM) is a critical driver of oxygen consumption and the formation of the large, seasonal hypoxic zone in the Northern Gulf of Mexico. However, bulk rate measurements and genomic inferences cannot definitively link specific bacterial and archaeal taxa to the assimilation of complex DOM pools, limiting our mechanistic understanding of this biogeochemical 'dead' zone's dynamics. Resolving this link is essential for predicting how hypoxia will respond to changes in nutrient loads and climate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, phylogenetically defined bacterial clades, particularly those affiliated with Bacteroidetes and Gammaproteobacteria, will be the primary direct assimilators of Mississippi River-derived DOM, and that their cellular uptake rates will correlate with spatial gradients in hypoxia severity.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard incubations with 13C- and 15N-labeled natural DOM extracted from Mississippi River water. After incubation, samples from key oxygen zones (oxic, hypoxic, anoxic) will be fixed and subjected to Catalyzed Reporter Deposition-Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (CARD-FISH) using a suite of rRNA-targeted probes for dominant taxa. Hybridized cells will be analyzed via nano-scale secondary ion mass spectrometry (NanoSIMS) to quantify isotopic enrichment (13C/12C, 15N/14N) in individual, phylogenetically identified cells. Uptake rates will be calculated per cell and correlated with environmental parameters (O2, nutrients).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, single-cell measurements of DOM assimilation by defined microbial taxa in a major coastal hypoxic system. It will identify the key microbial players in DOM processing and quantify their metabolic activity, moving beyond genomic potential to actual function. This will refine carbon flow models for the Gulf and inform how microbial community shifts may modulate hypoxia under future scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While FISH-SIMS is established in soils and sediments, its application to quantify DOM uptake by specific planktonic microbes in a dynamic, environmentally relevant gradient like the Gulf hypoxic zone is novel. This adapts a powerful tool to solve the long-standing problem of linking identity to function in aquatic DOM cycling.

proposals.title: Linking Rare Taxa to Recalcitrant DOM Processing in the Northern Gulf of Mexico Hypoxic Zone via Single-Cell Isotope Probing and Genomics
proposals.motivation: In river-dominated shelves like the Northern Gulf of Mexico, hypoxia is driven by nutrient-fueled primary production and subsequent microbial respiration of labile organic matter. However, the fate of more recalcitrant dissolved organic matter (DOM) pools, which may influence long-term oxygen demand and carbon sequestration, is poorly understood. Rare microbial taxa are hypothesized to be critical for processing these complex substrates, but their specific identities and functional roles remain a 'black box' in ecosystem models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that phylogenetically rare microbial taxa in the Gulf's hypoxic zone and adjacent shelf zones are keystone functional agents, disproportionately responsible for the catabolism of chemically recalcitrant DOM compounds due to possession of unique catabolic gene suites not found in abundant community members.
proposals.method: We will combine microautoradiography-fluorescence in situ hybridization (MICRO-FISH) with single-cell genome sequencing. Water column samples from hypoxic and oxic zones will be incubated with isotopically (13C/15N) or fluorescently labeled model recalcitrant DOM substrates (e.g., aromatic compounds, complex polymers). Individual substrate-incorporating cells from rare phylogenetic groups (targeted by FISH probes for low-abundance clades) will be physically isolated via micromanipulation. Their genomes will be amplified and sequenced using established single-cell genomic pipelines (e.g., MDA, Illumina sequencing). Recovered genomes will be analyzed via metagenomic assembly and annotation (using tools like anvi'o and DRAM) to identify substrate-specific catabolic pathways and compare their prevalence against genomes from abundant taxa.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, genome-resolved evidence linking specific rare taxa to the processing of critical DOM compounds in a major coastal hypoxic system. It will move beyond correlation to establish mechanistic causation in DOM cycling, identifying the genetic 'toolkits' used. The results will refine microbial functional guilds in biogeochemical models for the Gulf, offering new insights into the drivers of hypoxia and carbon fate.
proposals.novelty: While single-cell genomics and substrate probing exist, their fusion to functionally target and genomically decode rare taxa actively consuming specific DOM in a complex environmental matrix is novel. This approach bypasses the averaging inherent in bulk 'omics, directly testing the 'kill-the-winner' and microbial loop hypotheses at the level of individual cells and genes.

proposals.title: Viral Keystone Predation as a Determinant of DOM Fate and Hypoxia Development on the Northern Gulf of Mexico Shelf
proposals.motivation: Summer hypoxia in the northern Gulf of Mexico is driven by microbial respiration of river-derived dissolved organic matter (DOM) and nutrients. Current models focus on bulk nutrient loading (Liebig's Law) but lack mechanistic understanding of how viral lysis, a major source of labile DOM and bacterial mortality, regulates the microbial community's metabolic function and thus oxygen demand. This gap limits our ability to predict hypoxia variability under changing climate and nutrient regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that viral lysis of specific, competitively dominant bacterial taxa acts as a keystone predation process, restructuring the DOM-degrading community and shifting the biochemical fate of DOM pools, thereby modulating the magnitude and timing of respiratory oxygen consumption.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard and laboratory mesocosm experiments using water from the Louisiana-Texas shelf. Treatments will manipulate viral pressure via size-fractionation (0.2 µm vs. 0.02 µm filters) and viral concentrates to create exclusions and enhancements. We will track community shifts using 16S rRNA and viral metagenomic sequencing, quantify DOM pool transformations via ultrahigh-resolution mass spectrometry (FT-ICR MS), and measure resultant oxygen consumption rates. The experimental design directly adapts keystone predator exclusion methods from macro-ecology to microbial systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel mechanistic link between viral ecology and biogeochemical hypoxia models. It will quantify how viral 'top-down' control interacts with 'bottom-up' nutrient loading to determine carbon cycling efficiency and oxygen demand. The results will improve the 'Hypoxia Index' framework by incorporating a key microbial interaction term.
proposals.novelty: Applying the formal ecological theory of keystone predation to viral-bacterial interactions in a biogeochemical context is novel. While viral lysis is known to shunt carbon, its role as a selective, community-structuring force that dictates the metabolic fate of terrestrial DOM in hypoxia formation remains untested.

proposals.title: Trait-Based Emergent Modeling of Microbial DOM Processing in the Northern Gulf of Mexico Hypoxic Zone: Linking Community Assembly to Carbon Cycling
proposals.motivation: The microbial processing of dissolved organic matter (DOM) is a critical but poorly constrained component of ocean carbon cycling, especially in hypoxic zones like the Northern Gulf of Mexico. Current models often use static, phylogenetically defined parameters, failing to capture the functional plasticity and redundancy of microbial communities in response to dynamic nutrient and oxygen gradients. This gap limits our ability to predict how carbon cycling will respond to climate-driven changes in hypoxia and riverine nutrient loading.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the emergent DOM consumption function of a microbial community can be predicted from a trait-based framework that simulates community assembly based on fundamental physiological trade-offs (e.g., growth yield vs. substrate affinity, oxygen tolerance vs. denitrification capacity), and that this emergent function will robustly capture observed biogeochemical rates across the Gulf's hypoxic zone.
proposals.method: We will develop an individual-based model where virtual microbes are defined by suites of quantifiable traits (e.g., maximum uptake rate, half-saturation constants, enzyme investment, respiratory pathways) drawn from pre-2026 culture and 'omics studies. Community assembly will be simulated via a resource competition framework (e.g., extending the `DEMENT` model concept) under gradients of DOM lability, nitrate, and oxygen informed by Gulf of Mexico time-series data. The emergent community-level DOM processing rates will be scaled for basin models using probabilistic trait distributions. Validation will leverage existing coupled metagenomic/metatranscriptomic and biogeochemical datasets from the Gulf.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, trait-based link between microbial community ecology and ocean carbon cycling models. It will yield a novel modeling framework that predicts microbial metabolic responses to environmental perturbations like expanding hypoxia, moving beyond descriptive correlations. The resulting scalable functional parameters will directly improve the representation of the microbial carbon pump in regional and global biogeochemical models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the modeling paradigm from static taxonomic guilds to emergent function based on physiological trade-offs, explicitly simulating community assembly as a driver of carbon processing. This integrates microbial ecology theory with ocean biogeochemistry in a way that directly addresses the functional redundancy and plasticity observed in 'omics data but absent in most pre-2026 models.

proposals.title: Linking Genomic Biogeography to Carbon Flux: A Mechanistic Framework for Modeling DOM Cycling on the Northern Gulf of Mexico Shelf
proposals.motivation: Dissolved organic matter (DOM) cycling is a key but poorly constrained component of the ocean carbon cycle, with microbial community composition critically influencing remineralization rates. Large-scale biogeochemical models, such as those used to project hypoxia in systems like the Gulf of Mexico, typically represent microbial processes with generalized, static parameters, limiting their predictive power under changing nutrient loads and climate. Integrating the genomic basis of microbial metabolic traits into these models is essential for advancing mechanistic understanding and improving forecasts of coastal hypoxia.
proposals.hypothesis: Genomic features predictive of a bacterial strain's isolation location encode DOM-uptake traits that, when mapped to specific environmental gradients, will yield location-specific parameterizations that significantly improve the simulation of DOM consumption and oxygen demand in a shelf-sea biogeochemical model.
proposals.method: We will train a convolutional neural network (CNN) to inversely predict the isolation province (e.g., river plume, hypoxic zone, outer shelf) of bacterial genomes from the Gulf of Mexico. Salient genomic features identified by the CNN—such as transporter genes—will be linked to DOM substrate affinities and maximum uptake rates via manual curation and existing databases (e.g., KEGG, TCDB). These trait-location relationships will be used to generate dynamic, spatially explicit parameter fields for a coupled physical-biogeochemical model (e.g., ROMS-NEMURO or a regional FVCOM implementation) of the northern Gulf, explicitly simulating DOM pools and microbial consumption.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first modeling framework that dynamically parameterizes DOM cycling based on predicted genomic biogeography, moving beyond static microbial functional groups. It will provide a novel, mechanistic explanation for spatial and temporal variability in DOM lability and oxygen consumption on the Gulf of Mexico shelf, directly informing hypoxia forecasts. The approach establishes a transferable paradigm for integrating genomic 'omics data into regional and global carbon cycle models.
proposals.novelty: While microbial genomics and biogeochemical modeling are mature fields, their integration has been limited. This proposal's novelty lies in using an inverse prediction model (genome to location) as a direct conduit to inform the spatial parameterization of a process-based DOM model, creating a closed loop from sequence data to ecosystem function.

proposals.title: Perturbing the Substrate Filter: Chemostat Experiments to Disentangle DOM Quality and Bacterial Community Assembly in Coastal Hypoxic Zones
proposals.motivation: In river-dominated shelves like the northern Gulf of Mexico, hypoxia is fueled by nutrient-driven productivity and subsequent microbial respiration of organic matter. However, the mechanistic link between the chemical composition of dissolved organic matter (DOM) and the assembly of bacterial communities that drive oxygen consumption remains correlative. Resolving this causal link is critical for predicting how shifts in land-derived DOM, as suggested by 'Liebig’s Law and Haber’s Tragedy', will alter microbial function and hypoxia severity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific DOM chemical classes (e.g., peptides, carbohydrates, aromatic compounds) act as primary selective filters, causally determining bacterial community phylogenetic structure and functional potential for carbon processing and nitrogen transformation, independent of subsequent community-driven DOM alteration.
proposals.method: We will conduct replicated chemostat experiments inoculating Gulf of Mexico shelf water communities into defined media. Treatments will involve systematic chemical perturbations of the DOM pool via additions of specific, isotopically labeled substrates (e.g., 13C-amino acids, 15N-chitin, lignin derivatives) or DOM fractions isolated via solid-phase extraction. Time-series response will be tracked using high-throughput 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing, metatranscriptomics, and ultrahigh-resolution mass spectrometry (FT-ICR MS). Community assembly will be analyzed using null modeling and phylogenetic bin-based methods to distinguish deterministic selection from stochastic processes.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from correlative observations to a causal, mechanistic framework for DOM-bacteria interactions. It will directly test how 'giving the land a kick' through specific DOM inputs filters for microbial taxa and functions relevant to carbon fate and nitrogen cycling in coastal systems, thereby refining models of hypoxia development.
proposals.novelty: The experimental design explicitly decouples the initial selective pressure of DOM quality from the confounding feedback of community-driven DOM transformation, a major limitation of prior observational and batch culture studies. This allows for a true test of DOM as a causal driver in community assembly.

ideas.title: Develop a Trait-Based Genomic Module for Ocean Biogeochemical Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from terrestrial plant ecology, where models use Plant Functional Types defined by physiological traits to predict ecosystem fluxes. The analogous problem of scaling from individual organism characteristics to ecosystem-level processes in oceanography is solved by defining Microbial Functional Genomic Types (MFGTs). Key genomic traits—such as genes for specific metabolic pathways, nutrient transporters, and stress responses—are identified from sequenced marine microbial strains and aggregated into MFGT categories. These trait sets are then parameterized into mathematical functions governing growth, resource use, and mortality within ocean biogeochemical models. This creates a modular, trait-based genomic layer that informs model dynamics, moving beyond broad taxonomic groups to a more mechanistic, genome-informed prediction of ocean ecosystem function and biogeochemical cycles.

ideas.title: Genome-Informed, Location-Aware Modeling of Oceanic DOM Cycling
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel modeling framework that integrates the inverse prediction of a strain's isolation location from its genome (Idea B) with large-scale models of dissolved organic matter (DOM) consumption (Idea A). The synergy is achieved by using the location-prediction models to map genomic features—which encode DOM uptake traits—to specific oceanic provinces and environmental gradients. This allows the generation of location-specific, phylogenetically informed parameter fields for DOM uptake, transforming large-scale carbon cycle models from using generalized microbial functional groups to being dynamically informed by the predicted genomic biogeography of microbial communities. Consequently, the models can simulate DOM cycling with greater mechanistic fidelity and can project changes under novel environmental conditions by predicting how microbial genomic traits, and thus their biogeochemical functions, would redistribute.

ideas.title: Experimental perturbation of DOM pools to resolve causal drivers of bacterial community assembly and function
ideas.description: This study uses controlled chemostat experiments to causally test how dissolved organic matter (DOM) composition shapes bacterial communities. Replicate communities are subjected to defined chemical perturbations of the DOM pool, while high-resolution time-series 'omics and chemistry track the community's phylogenetic and functional response. This design explicitly separates the causal effect of DOM availability from the confounding feedback of community-driven DOM transformation. The result is a mechanistic, rather than correlative, understanding of how specific DOM qualities act as selective filters to structure aquatic microbial assemblages.

ideas.title: Single-Cell Genomic Mapping of Functional Niche Partitioning by Rare Taxa in DOM Cycling
ideas.description: This research direction combines MICRO-FISH with single-cell genome sequencing to target and genomically profile rare microbial taxa within a complex community. It then directly tests their disproportionate role in consuming specific, critical dissolved organic matter (DOM) compounds, such as those that are chemically recalcitrant or essential for ecosystem processes. By physically isolating single cells from rare phylogenetic groups that have incorporated isotopically or fluorescently labeled DOM substrates, we can sequence their genomes to identify the precise catabolic genes they employ. This approach definitively establishes whether low-abundance taxa act as keystone functional agents by possessing unique genetic machinery for substrate utilization that is not prevalent in the abundant majority.

ideas.title: Mapping the intracellular functional landscape of microbial DOM processing
ideas.description: This research direction applies the spatial statistics and patch-matrix-corridor frameworks of landscape ecology to analyze single-cell expression data for extracellular enzymes involved in dissolved organic matter (DOM) degradation. Instead of mapping genomic diversity across sampling sites, we map the 'functional expression landscapes' of microbial communities, treating individual cells with distinct enzyme expression profiles as patches within a chemical and physical seascape. This allows us to quantify how environmental substrate gradients and patchiness shape the connectivity, resilience, and functional heterogeneity of microbial metabolic networks at the intracellular scale, predicting community-level biogeochemical function from the emergent spatial patterning of single-cell physiological states.

ideas.title: Testing DOM utilization with isotopic pulse-chase and resource choice experiments
ideas.description: This idea challenges the single-substrate experimental design assumption by adapting a solution from terrestrial plant ecology. In that field, ecologists use dual-isotope pulse-chase labeling of different organic matter sources to trace plant nutrient preferences in complex, mixed-resource environments. Adapted to microbial ecology, the research would involve simultaneously presenting natural bacterial communities with two distinct Dissolved Organic Matter (DOM) sources (e.g., ¹³C-labeled algal exudate and ²H-labeled leaf litter leachate). Through time-series sampling, the incorporation of each isotopic label into bacterial biomass (via PLFA-SIP or nucleic acid-SIP) and respired CO₂ would be measured. This approach directly tests how substrate utilization preferences, including preferential uptake, co-metabolism, and metabolic switching, change when bacteria are presented with mixed DOM sources, moving beyond single-substrate assays to a more environmentally realistic experimental framework.

ideas.title: Applying Keystone Predation Theory to Viral-Bacterial Interactions in DOM Cycling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the keystone predation framework from community ecology to microbial systems. The analogous problem—understanding how a predator's selective pressure reshapes prey community function and resource cycling—was solved through controlled exclusion experiments and mesocosm studies. Adapted here, the research will investigate how viral lysis of specific dissolved organic matter (DOM)-consuming bacteria acts as a keystone process. By creating targeted viral 'predation' treatments (exclusions or enhancements) in defined microbial communities, the study will isolate how viral pressure alters the competitive landscape among DOM utilizers, leading to measurable shifts in community metabolic function and the biochemical fate of DOM pools.

ideas.title: Phylogeographic Source Attribution for Marine Microbes Using Biogeochemical Tracers
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from forensic geology and provenance analysis to the microbial oceanography inverse problem. In the analogous field, the geographic origin of a material is predicted by matching its geochemical or isotopic signature to spatial maps of those traits. Here, we propose to treat the microbial genome as containing a biochemical 'signature of place.' The solution involves identifying genomic features—such as genes for transporters, enzymes, or regulatory systems that are adaptive to specific environmental parameters (e.g., temperature regimes, phosphate scarcity, iron availability)—that serve as proxies for local oceanic conditions. A machine learning model (e.g., a convolutional neural network on k-mer spectra or a classifier on a curated set of adaptive markers) will be trained on a global collection of genomes with known isolation coordinates. The model will learn the complex mapping between these genomic 'tracers' and geographic location, ultimately enabling the prediction of the oceanic isolation location of a novel strain from its genome sequence alone, scaling with both genomic data volume and environmental parameter dimensionality.

ideas.title: Mapping the functional network of complete DOM degradation across the microbial phylogeny
ideas.description: This research will employ an expanded MICRO-FISH platform, targeting a broad phylogenetic range of key bacterial groups (e.g., Bacteroidetes, Planctomycetes, SAR11, Gammaproteobacteria), to track the simultaneous uptake of numerous structurally distinct DOM compounds within the same environmental sample. By correlating high-resolution phylogenetic identity with the consumption profiles of individual components from a complex DOM pool, we will construct a functional network map. This map will reveal metabolic niches, potential cross-feeding interactions, and identify which phylogenetic groups are essential for initiating degradation versus mineralizing refractory intermediates, thereby elucidating the collective microbial strategy for complete DOM turnover in situ.

ideas.title: Adapt NanoSIMS with FISH for single-cell DOM uptake phylogeny
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from soil microbial ecology, where FISH-SIMS is used to link identity and function in complex communities, to the aquatic DOM cycling context. The analogous problem of quantifying substrate use by specific taxa in an environmental mixture was solved by combining phylogenetic staining (FISH) with nanoscale isotopic measurement (NanoSIMS). The adapted research idea involves incubating aquatic microbial communities with isotopically labeled DOM, using fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) to tag specific bacterial or archaeal taxa, and then applying NanoSIMS to quantify the isotopic enrichment (e.g., 13C, 15N) from the labeled DOM per individual, phylogenetically identified cell. This will directly measure which taxa assimilate DOM and at what rate, moving beyond bulk or community-level measurements.

ideas.title: Decoupling concentration depletion from substrate preference in mixed DOM utilization
ideas.description: This study uses chemostat-based continuous culture to overcome the confounding effect of substrate depletion in batch experiments. By maintaining constant, non-limiting concentrations of two or more chemically distinct dissolved organic matter (DOM) substrates, we can measure real-time uptake kinetics and biomass production for each component simultaneously. This approach directly tests whether bacteria exhibit a fixed hierarchy of preferences or flexible, concentration-dependent co-metabolism when substrates are continuously available, as in many natural environments. The system isolates true metabolic regulation from the artifacts of sequential depletion.

ideas.title: Disentangling the relative contributions of horizontal gene transfer, host regulation, and ecological filtering to dissolved organic matter niche partitioning
ideas.description: This study moves beyond correlative assessments of HGT to establish causal links between gene acquisition and ecosystem function. We integrate pangenomics, transcriptomics, and metabolic modeling to distinguish 'silent' HGT events from those that are functionally integrated and expressed in response to specific DOM compounds. The core experimental system uses genetically tractable marine bacterial isolates as recipients for conjugative transfer of putative DOM utilization operons, allowing direct testing of whether acquisition alters niche boundaries in controlled chemostat competition experiments. This approach quantifies the conditional fitness contributions of HGT events within specific phylogenetic and environmental contexts.

ideas.title: Trait-based emergent modeling of microbial DOM processing for ocean carbon cycling
ideas.description: This approach shifts from static phylogenetic parameters to modeling the emergent DOM consumption function of microbial communities based on fundamental physiological trade-offs. A trait-based framework simulates community assembly and function in response to environmental gradients, capturing plasticity and redundancy. This emergent functional output is then scaled for integration into ocean basin carbon models via probabilistic distributions of key traits. The system is rigorously validated against coupled ‘omics and biogeochemical datasets to ensure it robustly predicts community metabolic responses to environmental perturbations.

By applying isotope-labeled DOM substrates to samples and then using NanoSIMS to measure uptake in taxonomically identified cells (e.g., via FISH or after cell sorting), we can directly test whether high DOM incorporation correlates with phylogenetic lineage or, instead, with the presence of specific horizontally acquired gene clusters. This moves beyond correlative studies to provide causal, mechanistic evidence for the role of HGT in shaping microbial biogeochemical function.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Construction Theory to DOM-Bacterial Systems
ideas.description: This research expands the original inverse problem by drawing an analogy from ecological niche construction theory. In macroecology, the solution to understanding how organisms shape their environment and are in turn shaped by it involves tracking environmental modifications and their evolutionary consequences. Adapted to microbial ecology, the study will determine how the initial available DOM pool selects for a bacterial phylogenetic assemblage, then how this active assemblage enzymatically transforms the DOM composition (niche construction). The core inverse problem is thus scaled to investigate how this newly constructed DOM pool subsequently selects for and structures the next iteration of the active bacterial community, revealing a dynamic, dual-scaled feedback mechanism between chemical resource landscapes and phylogenetic diversity.

ideas.title: Adapting Spatial Transcriptomics to Link Microbial Genomes to Substrate Use
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from spatial transcriptomics in cancer and neurobiology to microbial ecology. In those fields, spatial transcriptomics preserves tissue architecture and uses imaging to identify cells of a specific phenotype (e.g., tumor region, neuronal type) before sequencing their mRNA in situ to link location/function to gene expression. Here, we adapt this by using MICRO-FISH imaging to first identify the specific microbial cells within an environmental sample that are actively utilizing a given dissolved organic matter (DOM) substrate. Subsequently, on the same physically fixed sample, we perform in situ single-cell genome sequencing (e situ sequencing or a similar method) to obtain the genome from those exact, functionally characterized cells. This directly links a measurable in situ metabolic function (substrate uptake) to the genetic repertoire of the individual cell performing it, overcoming the classic disconnect between activity assays and genomic assignment in complex microbial communities.

ideas.title: Viral Predation and Functional Rescue: Single-Cell Genomic Tracking of DOM Cycling Shifts
ideas.description: This research direction combines viral manipulation experiments with MICRO-FISH and single-cell genome sequencing to track how viral lysis of key DOM-utilizing bacteria alters the functional landscape. By isolating and sequencing the genomes of individual cells that actively take up specific DOM substrates before and after viral perturbation, we can identify which taxa and genetic pathways functionally replace the lysed populations. This approach directly links the loss of specific host-associated genes to the emergence of alternative genetic strategies for DOM processing within the community. The result is a mechanistic, gene-resolved understanding of how viral predation acts as a dynamic evolutionary force shaping the metabolic network responsible for carbon cycling.

ideas.title: Functional Guilds Over Phylogenetic Lineages: A Materials Science Analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that phylogenetic clades are the primary functional units in microbial ecology by drawing an analogy from materials science. There, function is determined by specific structural arrangements (e.g., perovskite crystal structures) that can arise from different elemental compositions, not by the elements' phylogenetic relationships. Adapting this solution, the research would investigate if ecological function is better defined by horizontally transferable 'functional gene network architectures'—analogous to material prototypes—than by taxonomic clade. The project would develop bioinformatic methods to identify these core, conserved functional modules from metagenomic and genomic data and rigorously test their predictive power for environmental parameters and biogeochemical process rates against predictions made from standard phylogenetic classifications.

ideas.title: Dual-Experimental: Causally Testing Genomic Loss as an Adaptive Driver in Oligotrophy
ideas.description: This project moves beyond correlation by experimentally testing the adaptive value of gene loss. Using a model marine cyanobacterium, we will perform long-term evolution experiments under defined oligotrophic conditions to observe deadaptive gene inactivation events. In parallel, we will construct knockout mutants of genes commonly lost in natural populations and directly measure their competitive fitness in nutrient-limited chemostats. This combined approach will establish causal links between specific genetic deletions and increased fitness in low-nutrient environments, definitively separating adaptive genome streamlining from neutral genetic drift.

ideas.title: Eco-Fluidic Single-Cell Assay: Linking DOM Processing to Dynamic Environmental Complexity
ideas.description: This system couples continuous cultivation of natural microbial consortia under fluctuating, environmentally relevant conditions with high-throughput microfluidic single-cell analysis. Cells are encapsulated in droplets with complex natural DOM mixtures, and the device monitors dynamic physicochemical changes within each droplet in real-time. This allows for the direct correlation of single-cell uptake and transformation phenotypes with the specific chemical features of the DOM and the concurrent environmental context. By preserving ecological complexity from cultivation through measurement, the approach reveals how environmental dynamics shape the functional heterogeneity of DOM-processing bacteria.

ideas.title: Direct causal testing of rare taxa as keystone DOM consumers via dilution extinction and isotope pool partitioning
ideas.description: This study uses a two-phase experimental system to causally test the role of rare phylogenetic groups in critical DOM processing. First, microcosms with 13C-labeled DOM compounds are monitored via coupled metagenomics, metatranscriptomics, and metabolomics to identify candidate rare taxa actively assimilating the label. Second, sequential dilution-to-extinction treatments are applied to physically remove rare populations, followed by re-spiking with the same labeled DOM to track if the consumption kinetics, transformation pathways, and isotopic incorporation into biomass are disproportionately altered compared to untreated controls. This approach moves beyond correlation to establish if the functional capacity for consuming specific DOM is lost with the removal of rare groups.

ideas.title: Network Analysis of Metabolic Specialization in Microbial Consortia
ideas.description: This research fuses the ecological hypothesis about DOM utilization with network analysis of gene co-occurrence to investigate how microbial metabolic strategies are encoded and co-inherited. By constructing gene co-occurrence networks from COG data across diverse environments, we will identify network modules representing 'generalist' (complex DOM-degrading) and 'specialist' (simple monomer-targeting) gene suites. This allows us to test if these metabolic strategies form distinct, co-inherited genetic modules and predict the environmental conditions (e.g., DOM complexity gradients) that select for their prevalence in communities. The approach transforms a phenotypic ecological hypothesis into a testable genomic framework, linking community-level resource partitioning to underlying patterns of gene inheritance and co-evolution.

ideas.title: Seascape-Aware Geostatistical Framework for Genomic Diversity
ideas.description: This framework integrates oceanographic connectivity models with spatial genetics to overcome the misapplication of terrestrial landscape metrics to marine systems. It uses hydrodynamic and particle dispersal models to generate continuous, probabilistic seascape layers that define realistic pathways for gene flow. Genomic diversity data from sampling sites are then analyzed using geostatistical methods conditioned on these physical connectivity priors, explicitly modeling diversity as a continuous field in 3D ocean space. This produces robust, process-informed maps of genomic variation that accurately reflect the influence of ocean currents, depth, and other fluid dynamic forces on population structure.

ideas.title: Phylogenetically-Broad Assessment of Niche Partitioning and Metabolic Flexibility in Complex DOM Mixtures
ideas.description: This research direction applies MICRO-FISH to phylogenetically diverse groups (e.g., Planctomycetes, SAR11, Bacteroidetes, Gammaproteobacteria) but crucially challenges them with defined mixtures of DOM substrates, rather than single compounds. By doing so, it tests the fundamental hypothesis that substrate utilization hierarchies and metabolic strategies (e.g., simultaneous co-utilization vs. strict preference) are phylogenetically constrained traits. The synergy creates a framework to map 'interaction niches'—determining if groups shift from specialists to generalists in mixtures or engage in predictable cross-feeding—thereby linking phylogenetic identity to function in the complex resource landscapes of natural environments.

ideas.title: Apply geostatistical kriging from mineral prospecting to interpolate genomic diversity
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from geostatistics and mineral prospecting—kriging interpolation—to the analysis of genomic diversity maps. In the analogous field, sparse point measurements (e.g., ore grade from drill cores) are used to model a continuous spatial field, quantifying spatial structure via variograms to make optimal predictions with known uncertainty. Adapted to genomic ocean data, this method would treat sampling sites as 'cores' and genomic diversity metrics as the 'ore grade.' By applying kriging, researchers can create predictive, continuous surfaces of genetic diversity across the seascape, formally account for spatial autocorrelation in genetic data, and produce maps that highlight regions of high uncertainty to efficiently direct future ocean genomic surveys.

ideas.description: This research abandons the simplistic binary classification of DOM in favor of a continuous, multi-dimensional chemical characterization using high-resolution analytical techniques. It directly measures microbial functional traits—such as genomic potential for substrate uptake and catabolism—from the same environmental samples or controlled microcosms. By applying multivariate statistical and network analyses, the project tests for predictable correlations between specific axes of DOM chemistry and the functional profiles of microbial taxa, moving beyond a generalist-specialist dichotomy to map the full spectrum of metabolic strategies.

ideas.title: Link DOM assimilation phenotypes to population genetic structure using single-cell NanoSIMS and pangenomics
ideas.description: This research direction combines NanoSIMS-based single-cell measurement of DOM assimilation with population genetics analysis of pangenomes from the same Prochlorococcus populations. By performing NanoSIMS on isotopically labeled DOM substrates for individual cells that are then sorted and sequenced, we can quantify DOM incorporation per cell and directly link this phenotypic trait to the genomic background (core and accessory genes) of that specific cell. Applying population genetics statistics (e.g., FST) to genomic clusters defined by their measured DOM assimilation phenotypes will reveal whether physiological niches, quantified at the single-cell level, correspond to genetically distinct sub-populations. This approach tests the hypothesis that differential resource utilization is a key selective force structuring microbial pangenomes and driving ecological divergence.

ideas.title: A multi-scale variance partitioning framework to disentangle phylogenetic, core genome, and accessory genome signals in ecological function
ideas.description: This research systematically quantifies the relative contributions of phylogenetic history, conserved core genes, and variable accessory genes in predicting microbial ecological function. Instead of a binary comparison, the study employs a nested sampling design across taxonomic ranks and uses statistical models to partition the variance in functional traits explained by each factor. This approach reveals whether function is best predicted by deep evolutionary history (phylogeny), by the stable genetic backbone of a group (core genome), or by recent horizontal gene transfer and gene loss (accessory genome), and how these signals interact across scales. The result is a more nuanced model of functional prediction that integrates rather than opposes phylogenetic and gene-centric perspectives.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Micromanipulation for Rare Prochlorococcus Clades
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from astrophysics, where coronagraphs suppress bright starlight to reveal faint exoplanets, to the challenge of studying rare Prochlorococcus clades obscured by abundant ones. The analogous solution involves direct imaging via starlight suppression. Adapted to microbiology, the research would develop a microfluidic device that uses clade-specific FISH labeling to first identify target cells. The device would then implement a form of 'biological signal suppression'—perhaps via targeted lysis of abundant, non-target cells or precise optical/fluidic sorting—to effectively clear the dominant population, enabling the clean isolation and subsequent single-cell genomic sequencing of individual cells from the rare, uncultured clade of interest.

ideas.title: Single-cell resolved tracing of defined DOM compound classes into phylogenetically identified bacteria in situ
ideas.description: This approach applies 13C-labeled versions of specific, environmentally relevant DOM compound classes (not bulk DOM) in situ via substrate-amendment experiments. After incubation, single cells that have incorporated the label are rapidly sorted via fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) based on their isotopic enrichment. Phylogenetic identity of active cells is determined by 16S rRNA gene sequencing of sorted populations or, where possible, via optical photothermal infrared (O-PTIR) spectroscopy of single cells. This system directly links the uptake and utilization of a known DOM substrate class to specific microbial taxa within the complex natural assemblage, providing a quantifiable and phylogenetically resolved picture of metabolic partitioning.

ideas.title: Network-Based Analysis of DOM Utilization: Decoupling Phylogeny from Functional Trait Distribution
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of phylogenetic determinism in DOM utilization by drawing an analogy from microbial ecology's study of antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs). In that field, the analogous problem of distinguishing phylogenetic inheritance from horizontal gene transfer in shaping functional trait distribution was solved by constructing gene-sharing networks and applying network analysis metrics (e.g., modularity, centrality) to quantify the contribution of HGT. We adapt this solution by proposing a network-based analysis of DOM utilization. The research would construct a bipartite network linking microbial taxa (from genomic data) to specific DOM utilization genes (e.g., for complex polymer breakdown, transporter families). Using network metrics, we would statistically partition the variance in DOM utilization patterns, explicitly testing whether the network's structure shows high modularity aligned with phylogeny (supporting determinism) or high connectivity and cross-clade gene sharing (supporting HGT as a primary driver). This approach provides a quantitative framework to relax the condition of purely phylogenetic determinism.

ideas.title: Raman-Activated Cell Sorting Coupled to NanoSIMS for Dynamic, Multi-Modal Tracking of DOM Processing by Single Cells
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the static, destructive limitation of standalone NanoSIMS by first using Raman-activated cell sorting (RACS) to non-destructively identify and sort live microbial cells based on spectral evidence of isotopic label incorporation from DOM substrates. The sorted populations, which can be linked to specific metabolic states via their inherent Raman fingerprints, are then analyzed by NanoSIMS for precise, taxon-specific quantification of isotopic enrichment per cell. This dual-mode system allows for time-series tracking of DOM uptake dynamics in sub-populations and directly links high-resolution isotopic measurements with the potential for subsequent genomic or cultivation-based analyses on sister cells from the same sorted pool.

ideas.title: Coupling single-cell enzyme expression with direct extracellular activity measurement during substrate shifts
ideas.description: This redesigned approach simultaneously quantifies both the intracellular expression dynamics of extracellular enzymes and their resulting functional activity in the pericellular space at single-cell resolution. It uses a dual-reporter system where one fluorescent protein reports on enzyme gene expression, while a distinct fluorophore is generated by the cleavage of a membrane-impermeable fluorogenic substrate analog by the secreted enzyme. This allows direct correlation between transcriptional response and actual degradation function for individual cells within a mixed microbial community experiencing substrate shifts, closing the loop from genetic potential to biogeochemical consequence.

ideas.title: Phylogenetically-controlled network analysis of functional gene co-dependency
ideas.description: This approach integrates species phylogeny directly into the network construction to distinguish functional gene associations from phylogenetic artifacts. It first models the expected pattern of gene presence/absence across genomes based on their evolutionary relationships, creating a phylogenetic null model. A network is then built from significant residual associations—connections that remain after accounting for shared ancestry—using methods like phylogenetic independent contrasts or phylogenetic logistic regression. This yields a network of gene modules that represent putative functional complexes, co-adaptive traits, or antagonistic relationships, providing a more robust and biologically interpretable map of co-inheritance.

ideas.title: Hypothesis-Driven Experimental Validation of COG Cluster Function
ideas.description: This approach begins with a computational phase that mines genomic context, protein structure predictions, and conserved domains to formulate specific, testable functional hypotheses for prioritized orthologous gene clusters. These hypotheses, such as involvement in a particular metabolic pathway or stress response, then guide a modular experimental validation pipeline. The pipeline employs targeted genetic manipulations in tractable model organisms coupled with phenotypic assays designed to confirm or refute the predicted function. This method ensures that validation is focused, efficient, and yields interpretable results that directly strengthen the functional annotation of the COG database.

ideas.title: Network-Informed Single-Cell Enzyme Expression Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction first applies network theory to genomic (COG) data from microbial communities to identify co-inherited modules of genes, specifically those containing extracellular enzyme genes and their potential regulatory partners. It then moves to the single-cell level, using techniques like microfluidics and single-cell enzyme activity assays or reporter constructs to measure the expression dynamics of the enzymes from the predicted network modules in individual cells. This creates a closed loop where network predictions of functional genetic units are empirically tested, revealing how co-inherited genetic potential is translated into heterogeneous, responsive phenotypic expression across a population, directly linking genomic architecture to cellular behavior in complex environments.

ideas.title: Microfluidic Single-Cell Enzyme Activity Reporter for Substrate Pulsing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology of single-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology from neuroscience to study intracellular enzyme expression dynamics in microbes. In the analogous field, a micropipette isolates a single neuron and delivers precise neurotransmitter pulses to measure ion channel response. Here, microfluidic cell traps replace the pipette to isolate individual bacterial or fungal cells. The system then delivers controlled, alternating pulses of different DOM substrates (e.g., protein vs. polysaccharide derivatives) to the trapped cell. By employing a fluorescent transcriptional reporter (e.g., GFP under the control of a promoter for an extracellular hydrolase), we can measure the timing, magnitude, and heterogeneity of per-cell enzyme expression in direct response to each substrate shift, scaling down population-level substrate degradation concepts to the precise decision-making of individual cells.

ideas.title: Adapting Community Detection from Social Networks to Identify Functional Gene Modules in COG Data
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from social network analysis to the genomic context. In social networks, community detection algorithms (e.g., Louvain method) are used to identify densely connected groups within a larger network, revealing latent structure. Applying this to COG data, we construct a network where nodes are Clusters of Orthologous Genes and edges represent significant co-occurrence across microbial genomes. By running community detection on this network, we can algorithmically identify modules of co-inherited genes. These modules likely correspond to functional complexes, conserved pathways, or evolutionary units that are frequently transferred or retained together, providing a data-driven map of genomic functional architecture beyond manual annotation.

ideas.title: Experimentally Validate Network-Predicted Functional Modules of Co-Inherited Genes
ideas.description: This research direction first applies network analysis to COG co-occurrence data to identify tightly connected modules of genes that are consistently co-inherited across genomes, suggesting they form functional complexes or pathways. These network-predicted modules, rather than individual orthologous clusters, become the primary units for experimental interrogation. Validation strategies, such as combinatorial gene knockouts, synthetic lethality screens, or co-expression analyses, are then designed to test the predicted functional synergy and essentiality of the module as a whole. This approach directly tests the evolutionary principle that co-inheritance predicts functional interaction, providing ground-truth data to refine network models and uncover higher-order genetic architecture.
